上卷.text: The 120 superior drugs serve as "rulers" (jun), governing the nourishing of life in correspondence with Heaven. They are non-toxic; taken in large amounts or over a long time, they do not harm a person. Those wishing to lighten the body, boost qi, resist aging, and prolong life should base themselves on the Upper Classic (上經).

The 120 middle drugs serve as "ministers" (chen), governing the nourishing of one's nature in correspondence with Man. Some are non-toxic, some are toxic — their suitability must be weighed with care. Those wishing to check disease and supplement deficiency and weakness should base themselves on the Middle Classic (中經).

The 125 inferior drugs serve as "assistants and envoys" (zuoshi), governing the treatment of disease in correspondence with Earth. Most are toxic and should not be taken over a long time. Those wishing to eliminate cold and heat pathogenic qi, break up accumulations and masses, and cure illness should base themselves on the Lower Classic (下經).

Drugs have the roles of ruler, minister, assistant, and envoy, combined so as to mutually direct and regulate one another. One should use one ruler, two ministers, and five assistants; alternatively, one ruler, three ministers, and nine assistants and envoys.

Drugs have yin-yang pairings, like mother and child, elder and younger brother — root, stem, flower, fruit, herb, mineral, bone, and flesh. There are those that act alone, those that mutually reinforce each other, those that mutually guide each other, those that mutually restrain each other, those that mutually dislike each other, those that mutually conflict with each other, and those that mutually suppress each other. These seven relationships must all be weighed when combining drugs: one should use those that mutually reinforce or mutually guide well, and avoid those that mutually dislike or conflict. If a drug is toxic, its toxicity should be moderated — one may use those that mutually restrain or mutually suppress it; otherwise they should not be combined. Drugs have the five flavors — sour, salty, sweet, bitter, and pungent — and the four natures — cold, hot, warm, and cool — as well as being toxic or non-toxic. Drying in the shade or in the sun, the timing of gathering and processing, whether used raw or prepared, the region of origin, and whether genuine or adulterated, old or fresh — each of these has its proper method.

By nature, some drugs are suited to being made into pills, some into powders, some to be boiled in water, some steeped in wine, some rendered into pastes by decoction; some substances suit several of these methods at once, and some must not be put into decoctions or wine. All must follow the nature of the drug — this must not be violated.

Whenever one wishes to treat disease, first examine its origin and observe the mechanism of the illness. If the five viscera are not yet depleted, the six bowels not yet exhausted, the blood vessels not yet in disorder, and the spirit not yet dispersed, then taking medicine will surely save the patient. If the disease has already become established, only half a cure may be achieved. If the course of the disease has already passed its critical point, life will be hard to preserve.

If toxic drugs are used to treat disease, begin with a dose as small as a millet grain, and stop once the disease is gone. If it is not gone, double the dose; if still not gone, increase it tenfold. Take the disappearance of the disease as the measure. Treat cold with hot drugs, and heat with cold drugs. For indigestion of food and drink, use drugs that induce vomiting or purging. For ghost-attachment and gu-poisoning, use toxic drugs. For carbuncles, swellings, sores, and tumors, use sore-treating drugs. For wind-damp, use wind-damp drugs. Each should follow what is appropriate to it.

When disease is above the chest and diaphragm, eat first and take the medicine afterward. When disease is below the heart and abdomen, take the medicine first and eat afterward. When disease is in the limbs and blood vessels, medicine should be taken on an empty stomach, in the morning. When disease is in the bone marrow, medicine should be taken after a full meal, at night.

The principal categories of major disease include: wind-stroke, cold-damage disorders, warm malaria, attack by pestilence, sudden turmoil, abdominal distension, edema, dysenteric disorders with diarrhea, obstruction of urination and defecation, running-piglet qi, ascending qi, cough with counterflow, vomiting, jaundice, wasting-thirst, retained fluids, food stagnation, hardened accumulations and masses, fright-evil, epilepsy, ghost-attachment, throat obstruction, toothache, deafness, blindness, wounds from metal weapons, sprains and fractures, carbuncles and swellings, malignant sores, hemorrhoids, fistulas, goiter and fistulous swellings; in men, the five taxations and seven damages, deficiency and emaciation; in women, vaginal discharge, flooding, and amenorrhea; and injuries from insects, snakes, and gu-poison. These are but the general outline, the root patterns — the branching variations within each should be traced and addressed according to their particular thread.

中卷.text: Minerals and Stones — Superior Grade

Jade Spring (yuquan). Taste sweet, nature neutral. Indicated for the hundred diseases of the five viscera. Softens the sinews, strengthens the bones, calms the hun and po (spirit-souls), builds flesh, and boosts qi. Taken long-term, it enables one to withstand cold and heat without hunger or thirst, and prevents aging — a substance of the immortals. If a person near death takes five catties of it, the complexion will not change even after three years. Also called Jade Tablet (yuzha).

Cinnabar (dansha). Taste sweet, nature slightly cold. Treats the hundred diseases of the body and five viscera. Nourishes the essence-spirit, calms the hun and po, boosts qi, brightens the eyes, and kills malevolent spirits and demons. Taken long-term, it grants communication with spirit intelligence and prevents aging. It can transform into mercury. Grows in mountain valleys.

Mercury (shuiyin). Taste pungent, nature cold. Indicated for scabies, itching, crusted sores, and baldness with sores; kills lice and mites in the skin, induces abortion, and clears heat. It neutralizes the toxicity of gold, silver, copper, and tin; when melted it can be restored into cinnabar. Taken long-term, it grants spirit-immortality and freedom from death. Grows in level ground.

Kongqing, hollow azurite. Taste sweet, nature cold. Indicated for blue-blindness and deafness. Brightens the eyes, frees the nine orifices, opens the blood vessels, and nourishes the essence-spirit. Taken long-term, it lightens the body and prolongs life without aging. It can transmute copper, iron, lead, and tin into gold. Grows in mountain valleys.

Zengqing, layered azurite. Taste sour, nature slightly cold. Indicated for eye pain; stops tearing, treats wind-bi (painful obstruction), benefits the joints, frees the nine orifices, and breaks up masses and accumulations. Taken long-term, it lightens the body and prevents aging. It can transmute [metals] into gold and copper. Grows in mountain valleys.

Baiqing, white azurite. Taste sweet, nature neutral. Brightens the eyes and frees the nine orifices; treats deafness and pathogenic qi below the heart. Induces vomiting and kills all manner of toxins and the three worms (intestinal parasites). Taken long-term, it grants communication with spirit intelligence, lightens the body, and prolongs life without aging. Grows in mountain valleys.

Mica (yunmu). Taste sweet, nature neutral. Treats dead flesh on the skin, and wind-stroke with chills and fever as if aboard a moving carriage or boat; expels pathogenic qi, calms the five viscera, benefits the essence of offspring, brightens the eyes, lightens the body, and prolongs life. Also called Cloud Pearl, Cloud Flower, Cloud Blossom, Cloud Fluid, Cloud Sand, and Scale Stone. Grows in mountain valleys.

Crude Mirabilite (puxiao). Taste bitter, nature cold, non-toxic. Indicated for the hundred diseases; expels cold and heat pathogenic qi, and drives out accumulations, hardened stagnation, and lingering blockages in the six bowels. It can transmute seventy-two kinds of minerals. Refined and taken as a preparation, it lightens the body — a substance of the immortals. Grows in mountain valleys.

Niter (xiaoshi). Taste bitter, nature cold. Indicated for accumulated heat in the five viscera, and distension and blockage of the stomach. It washes away stagnant accumulations of food and drink, expels the old to bring in the new, and clears pathogenic qi. Refined until paste-like; taken long-term, it lightens the body. Also called Mangxiao (Glauber's salt). Grows in mountain valleys.

Alum (fanshi). Taste sour, nature cold. Indicated for chills and fever with diarrhea, white vaginal discharge and genital erosion, malignant sores, and eye pain; hardens the bones and teeth. Refined and taken as a preparation, it lightens the body, prevents aging, and extends the years. Also called Yunie. Grows in mountain valleys.

Talc (huashi). Taste sweet, nature cold. Indicated for fever with diarrhea, difficult lactation in women, and urinary blockage. Promotes urination, washes away accumulated cold and heat in the stomach, and boosts the essence-qi. Taken long-term, it lightens the body, staves off hunger, and prolongs the years. Grows in the mountain valleys of Zheyang.

Purple Stone (zishi). Taste sweet, nature neutral. Indicated for cough and counterflow qi in the chest and abdomen; supplements deficiency, and treats wind-cold lodged in the uterus in women, and infertility of ten years' standing. Taken long-term, it warms the interior, lightens the body, and prolongs life. Grows in the mountain valleys of Mount Tai.

White Quartz (bai shiying). Taste sweet, nature slightly warm. Indicated for wasting-thirst, impotence and insufficiency, cough with counterflow qi, and long-standing cold between the chest and diaphragm. Boosts qi and dispels wind-damp painful obstruction. Taken long-term, it lightens the body and prolongs the years. Grows in mountain valleys.

Blue, Red, Yellow, White, and Black Stone-Fat (shizhi), and the like. Taste sweet, nature neutral. Indicated for jaundice, diarrhea, dysentery with pus and blood, genital erosion, red-and-white vaginal discharge, pathogenic qi, carbuncles and swellings, malignant sores with pus, sores on the head, and scabies with itching. Taken long-term, they supplement the marrow, boost qi, make one robust and vigorous, stave off hunger, lighten the body, and prolong the years. The five stone-fats each, according to their color, supplement one of the five viscera. Grow in mountain valleys.

Taiyi Yuyuliang (Great Unity's Yu's Surplus-Grain). Taste sweet, nature neutral. Indicated for cough with counterflow and ascending qi, masses and accumulations, amenorrhea, and irregular spotting; expels pathogenic qi. Taken long-term, it enables one to withstand cold and heat without hunger, lightens the body, and grants the power of flight over a thousand li — a substance of the immortals. Also called Stone Marrow (shinao). Grows in mountain valleys.

Yuyuliang (Yu's Surplus-Grain). Taste sweet, nature cold. Indicated for cough with counterflow, chills and fever, vexation and fullness, diarrhea with red and white discharge, and severe heat associated with amenorrhea and masses. Refined and taken as a preparation, it staves off hunger, lightens the body, and prolongs the years. Grows in ponds and marshes.

Xionghuang (Realgar). Taste: bitter, neutral. Indicated for cold-heat scrofula (rat fistula), malignant sores, welling-abscesses and hemorrhoids, and necrotic flesh. Kills spectral entities, evil demons and noxious qi, and the poison of the hundred insects; overcomes the five weapons. Refined and ingested, it lightens the body and confers transcendence. Also called Huangshishi. Grows in mountain valleys.
Minerals and Stones — Middle Grade

Shidan (Chalcanthite). Taste: sour, slightly cold. Brightens the eyes and treats eye pain, metal wounds and various forms of epilepsy with spasms, corroding pain of the female genitals, stone strangury with cold-heat, uterine bleeding, and various evil poisonous qi; enables conception. Refined into pills and taken, it prevents aging; taken long-term, it prolongs life and confers transcendence. It can transform iron into copper, and produce gold and silver. Also called Bishi. Grows in mountain valleys.

Shizhongru (Stalactite). Taste: sweet, warm. Indicated for cough with rising qi. Brightens the eyes and boosts essence, calms the five viscera, unblocks all the joints, benefits the nine orifices, and promotes lactation. Grows in mountain valleys.

Cihuang (Orpiment). Taste: pungent, neutral. Indicated for malignant sores, scalp baldness with scabs and scabies; kills poisonous insects, treats body itching, evil qi, and various poisons. Refined and taken long-term, it lightens the body, extends the years, and prevents aging. Grows in mountain valleys.

Yinnie. Taste: pungent, warm. Indicated for rotting wounds with stagnant blood, diarrhea with cold-heat, scrofula (rat fistula), and abdominal masses with bound qi. Also called Jiangshi. Grows in mountain valleys.

Konggongnie. Taste: pungent, warm. Indicated for food damage with indigestion, evil bound qi, malignant sores, welling-abscesses, fistulas and hemorrhoids; benefits the nine orifices and promotes lactation. Grows in mountain valleys.

Shiliuhuang (Sulfur). Taste: sour, warm; toxic. Indicated for corroding of the female genitals, welling-abscesses and hemorrhoids with malignant blood; strengthens the sinews and bones, treats scalp baldness. It can transform gold, silver, copper and iron into wondrous substances. Grows in mountain valleys.

Yangqishi (Actinolite). Taste: sour; non-toxic. Indicated for uterine flooding and spotting; breaks up blood in the uterus, abdominal masses with bound qi, abdominal pain with cold-heat, infertility, and impotence; supplements deficiency. Also called Baishi. Grows in the mountain valleys of Qishan.

Ningshuishi. Taste: pungent, cold. Indicated for body heat, abdominal accumulations with evil qi, a burning sensation in the skin as if scorched by fire, and vexation and fullness. Taken dissolved in water. Taken long-term, it staves off hunger. Also called Baishuishi. Grows in mountain valleys.

Cishi (Magnetite). Taste: pungent, cold. Indicated for generalized painful obstruction from wind-dampness, pain in the limbs and joints such that one cannot hold objects, and a chilled aching pain; relieves great heat, vexation and fullness, and deafness. Also called Xuanshi. Grows in mountain valleys.

Lishi. Taste: pungent, cold. Indicated for body heat; benefits the stomach and relieves vexation, boosts essence and brightens the eyes, breaks up accumulations, and expels the three worms. Also called Lizhishi. Grows in mountain valleys.

Changshi. Taste: pungent, cold. Indicated for body heat, cold reversal of the four limbs; disinhibits urination, unblocks the blood vessels, brightens the eyes and removes visual film and dim vision, expels the three worms, and kills gu poison. Taken long-term, it staves off hunger. Also called Fangshi. Grows in mountain valleys.
Fuqing. Taste: pungent. Indicated for gu poison, venomous snakebite, poisoning from vegetables and meat, and malignant sores. Also called Tuiqing. Grows in mountain valleys.

Tieluo (forged iron flakes). Taste: pungent, neutral. Indicated for wind-heat malignant sores, ulcers, welling-abscesses, sores with scabs and scabies, and qi lodged in the skin. Iron itself firms the flesh and increases tolerance of pain. Iron essence (Tiejing) brightens the eyes and transforms copper. Grows in level marshes.
Minerals and Stones — Lower Grade

Shigao (Gypsum). Taste: pungent, slightly cold. Indicated for wind-strike with cold-heat, rebellious qi below the heart with fright and panting, dry mouth and scorched tongue with labored breathing, hardness and pain in the abdomen; expels evil demons; used in childbirth, and for metal wounds. Grows in mountain valleys.
Qinglanggan. Taste: pungent, neutral. Indicated for body itching, burns, welling-abscess wounds, scabies with itching, and necrotic flesh. Also called Shizhu. Grows in level marshes.

Fanshi (Alum). Taste: pungent; toxic. Indicated for cold-heat scrofula (rat fistula), corroding sores, necrotic flesh, wind painful obstruction, hardness in the abdomen, and evil qi; relieves heat. Also called Qingfenshi, Lizhishi, or Guyangshi. Grows in mountain valleys.

Daizhe (Hematite). Taste: bitter, cold. Indicated for demonic infixation, thief wind, and gu poison; kills spectral entities and evil demons, treats poisonous evil qi in the abdomen, and red vaginal discharge with spotting in women. Also called Xuwan. Grows in mountain valleys.

Luxian. Taste: bitter, salty, cold. Indicated for great heat, wasting-thirst, mania and vexation; expels evil, and by inducing vomiting and purging treats gu poison; softens the flesh and skin. Rock salt (Dayan) induces vomiting. Rong salt (Rongyan) brightens the eyes, treats eye pain, boosts qi, strengthens the flesh and bones, and expels gu poison. Grows in ponds and marshes.

Bai'e (white clay). Taste: bitter, warm. Indicated for cold-heat abdominal masses in women, amenorrhea, accumulations, swelling and pain of the genitals, spotting, and infertility. Grows in mountain valleys.

Qiandan (red lead / minium). Taste: pungent, slightly cold. Indicated for cough with rebellious qi, stomach reflux, fright epilepsy and manic-withdrawal illness; relieves heat and directs qi downward. When refined and transformed, it reverts to become the Nine Radiances. Taken long-term, it opens communication with the spirit intelligence. Grows in level marshes.

Fenxi (lead powder). Taste: pungent, cold. Indicated for latent corpse-transmission and venomous stings; kills the three worms. The knob of a tin-bronze mirror treats amenorrhea in women, abdominal masses with hidden intestinal obstruction, and infertility. Also called Jiexi. Grows in the mountain valleys of Guiyang.

Shihui (lime). Taste: pungent, warm. Indicated for welling-abscesses and ulcers, scabies with itching, heat qi, malignant sores, and lai disease with loss of eyebrows; kills hemorrhoid worms, and removes moles and polyps. Also called Ehui. Grows in mountain valleys.

Donghui (winter ash). Taste: pungent, slightly warm. Indicated for moles; removes warts and polyps, corroding welling-abscesses, and scabies with itching. Also called Lihui. Grows in rivers and marshes.
Herbs — Superior Grade

Qingzhi (green Ganoderma). Taste: sour, neutral. Brightens the eyes, supplements the liver qi, calms the essence and ethereal soul, and fosters benevolence and forbearance. Eaten long-term, it lightens the body, prevents aging, and prolongs life, conferring transcendence. Also called Longzhi. Grows in mountain valleys.

Chizhi (red Ganoderma). Taste: bitter, neutral. Indicated for binding in the chest; boosts the heart qi, supplements the center, increases wisdom, and improves memory. Eaten long-term, it lightens the body, prevents aging, and prolongs life, conferring transcendence. Also called Danzhi. Grows in mountain valleys.

Yellow Zhi (yellow reishi fungus). Taste sweet, nature neutral. Indicated for the five pathogens of the heart and abdomen; boosts spleen qi, calms the spirit, and brings loyalty, trustworthiness, and harmony. Taken long-term, it lightens the body, prevents aging, and prolongs life like an immortal. Also called Golden Zhi. Grows in mountain valleys.

White Zhi. Taste pungent, nature neutral. Indicated for cough with rising qi; boosts lung qi, opens and clears the mouth and nose, strengthens the will and courage, and calms the corporeal soul (po). Taken long-term, it lightens the body, prevents aging, and prolongs life like an immortal. Also called Jade Zhi. Grows in mountain valleys.

Black Zhi. Taste salty, nature neutral. Indicated for dribbling urinary blockage; disinhibits the water passages, boosts kidney qi, opens the nine orifices, and sharpens hearing and perception. Taken long-term, it lightens the body, prevents aging, and prolongs life like an immortal. Also called Mysterious Zhi. Grows in mountain valleys.

Purple Zhi. Taste sweet, nature warm. Indicated for deafness; benefits the joints, preserves the spirit, boosts essence-qi, strengthens the sinews and bones, and improves complexion. Taken long-term, it lightens the body, prevents aging, and prolongs life like an immortal. Also called Wood Zhi. Grows in mountain valleys.

Tianmendong (Cochinchinese asparagus root). Taste bitter, nature neutral. Indicated for sudden wind-damp hemilateral bi-syndrome (numbness and paralysis); strengthens the bones and marrow, kills the three worms, and expels hidden corpse-influx. Taken long-term, it lightens the body, boosts qi, and prolongs life. Also called Dianle. Grows in mountain valleys.

Zhu (Atractylodes). Taste bitter, nature warm. Indicated for damp bi-syndrome, necrotic flesh, tetany, and jaundice; stops sweating, clears heat, aids digestion, and can be prepared as a decoction or pill. Taken long-term, it lightens the body, prolongs life, and dispels hunger. Also called Mountain Thistle. Grows in the mountain valleys of Mount Zheng.

Weiwei (Fragrant Solomon's Seal, Polygonatum odoratum). Taste sweet, nature neutral. Indicated for wind-stroke and sudden heat causing immobility, torn sinews and knotted flesh, and various deficiencies. Taken long-term, it removes dark blemishes on the face, gives the complexion a healthy sheen, and lightens the body while preventing aging. Grows in river valleys.

Dried Rehmannia Root. Taste sweet, nature cold. Indicated for fractures, sprains, severed sinews, and internal injury; expels blood bi-syndrome, fills the bone marrow, and builds flesh. Made into a decoction, it removes cold-heat accumulations and bi-syndrome. The fresh root is even better. Also called Marrow of the Earth. Grows in river marshes.

Changpu (Acorus, Sweet Flag). Taste pungent, nature warm. Indicated for wind-cold-damp bi-syndrome and cough with rising qi; opens the orifices of the heart, supplements the five viscera, opens the nine orifices, sharpens hearing and vision, and clears the voice. Taken long-term, it lightens the body, improves memory, dispels confusion, and prolongs life. Also called Changyang. Grows in ponds and marshes.

Yuanzhi (Polygala Root). Taste bitter, nature warm. Indicated for cough with rising qi and internal injury; supplements deficiency, expels pathogenic qi, disinhibits the nine orifices, sharpens intelligence, improves hearing and vision, aids memory, and strengthens the mind and doubles one's strength. Taken long-term, it lightens the body and prevents aging. Its leaves are called Xiaocao (Small Herb). Also called Yaorao or Xicao. Grows in river valleys.

Zexie (Alisma). Taste sweet, nature cold. Indicated for wind-cold-damp bi-syndrome and difficult lactation; disperses water retention, nourishes the five viscera, boosts qi and strength, and promotes healthy weight. Taken long-term, it sharpens hearing and vision, dispels hunger, prolongs life, lightens the body, gives the face a healthy glow, and, it is said, enables one to walk on water. Also called Shuixie, Mangyu, or Huxie. Grows in ponds and marshes.

Shuyu (Chinese Yam, Dioscorea). Taste sweet, slightly warm. Indicated for internal injury; supplements deficiency and emaciation, removes cold-heat pathogenic qi, supplements the center and boosts qi and strength, and builds flesh. Taken long-term, it sharpens hearing and vision, lightens the body, dispels hunger, and prolongs life. Also called Mountain Yam. Grows in mountain valleys.

Juhua (Chrysanthemum Flower). Taste bitter, nature neutral. Indicated for wind affecting the head, dizziness with swelling and pain, eyes that feel as if bulging out, tearing, necrotic and wasted skin, and aversion to wind-damp bi-syndrome. Taken long-term, it benefits the blood and qi, lightens the body, and resists aging while prolonging life. Also called Jiehua. Grows in river marshes.

Gancao (Licorice Root). Taste sweet, nature neutral. Indicated for cold-heat pathogenic qi in the five viscera and six bowels; strengthens the sinews and bones, builds flesh, doubles one's strength, treats wounds from metal blades and swelling of the legs, and resolves toxicity. Taken long-term, it lightens the body and prolongs life. Grows in river valleys.

Renshen (Ginseng). Taste sweet, slightly cold. Mainly supplements the five viscera, calms the spirit, settles the ethereal and corporeal souls (hun and po), stops fright palpitations, expels pathogenic qi, brightens the eyes, and opens the heart and sharpens the intellect. Also called Renxian or Guigai. Grows in mountain valleys.

Shihu (Dendrobium). Taste sweet, nature neutral. Indicated for internal injury; removes bi-syndrome, sends qi downward, and supplements the five viscera in cases of vacuity taxation and emaciation, and strengthens yin. Taken long-term, it thickens the stomach and intestines, lightens the body, and prolongs life. Also called Linlan. Grows in mountain valleys.

Shilongrui (Ranunculus sceleratus). Taste bitter, nature neutral. Indicated for wind-cold-damp bi-syndrome and pathogenic qi in the heart and abdomen; benefits the joints and relieves vexation and fullness. Taken long-term, it lightens the body, brightens the eyes, and prevents aging. Also called Luguoneng or Dishen. Grows in river marshes.

Niuxi (Achyranthes Root). Taste bitter (sour), nature neutral. Indicated for wilting and bi-syndrome from cold-damp, spasm and contracture of the limbs, and knee pain that prevents bending and stretching; expels stagnant blood and qi, treats burns and scalds, and induces abortion. Taken long-term, it lightens the body and resists aging. Also called Shibei. Grows in river valleys.

Duhuo (Doubleteeth Pubescent Angelica Root). Taste bitter, nature neutral, non-toxic. Indicated for conditions caused by wind-cold; treats wounds from metal blades, relieves pain, and treats running-piglet syndrome, epilepsy, tetany, and women's hernia-like abdominal masses. Taken long-term, it lightens the body and resists aging. Also called Qianghuo, Qiangqing, or Huqiang Shizhe. Grows in river valleys.

Shengma (Cimicifuga Root, Bugbane). Taste sweet, nature neutral. Mainly resolves the hundred poisons, kills the hundred kinds of ancient spirit-essences and calamity-bringing ghosts, and wards off epidemic pestilence, miasma, and gu-poison. Taken long-term, it prevents premature death, lightens the body, and prolongs life. Also called Zhou Shengma. Grows in mountain valleys.

Chaihu (Bupleurum Root). Taste bitter, nature neutral. Indicated for bound qi in the heart, abdomen, intestines, and stomach, accumulation of food and drink, and cold-heat pathogenic qi; expels the old and brings in the new. Taken long-term, it lightens the body, brightens the eyes, and boosts essence. Also called Dixun. Grows in river valleys.

Fangkui. Taste pungent, nature cold. Indicated for hernia-like abdominal masses and intestinal diarrhea, bladder heat-binding with inhibited urination, cough with rising qi, warm malaria, epilepsy, and fright-mania with manic wandering. Taken long-term, it strengthens the bone marrow, boosts qi, and lightens the body. Also called Ligai. Grows in river valleys.

Shishi (Yarrow Seed, Achillea). Taste bitter, nature neutral. Indicated for impotence; boosts qi, fills out the flesh and skin, brightens the eyes, and sharpens intelligence and foresight. Taken long-term, it dispels hunger, prevents aging, and lightens the body. Grows in river valleys.

Yanlvzi. Taste bitter, slightly warm, non-toxic. Indicated for stagnant blood in the five viscera, water-qi in the abdomen, abdominal distension with lingering heat, wind-cold-damp bi-syndrome, and various bodily pains. Taken long-term, it lightens the body, prolongs life, and prevents aging. Grows in river valleys.

Yiyiren (Job's Tears, Coix Seed). Taste sweet, slightly cold. Indicated for tense sinews and contracture that prevent bending and stretching, and wind-damp bi-syndrome; sends qi downward. Taken long-term, it lightens the body and boosts qi. Its root expels the three worms. Also called Jieli. Grows in level marshes.

Cheqianzi (Plantago Seed). Taste sweet, nature cold, non-toxic. Indicated for qi dribbling-blockage; relieves pain, disinhibits the water passages and urination, and removes damp bi-syndrome. Taken long-term, it lightens the body and resists aging. Also called Dangtong. Grows in level marshes.

Ximingzi (Thlaspi/pepperweed seed). Taste: pungent, sour; nature: warm; non-toxic. Indicated for brightening vision, eye pain with tearing; removes painful obstruction, tonifies the five viscera, benefits essence and eyesight. Taken long-term, lightens the body and prevents aging. Also called Mixi, Daji, Maxin. Grows in mountain marshes.

Chongweizi (motherwort seed). Taste: pungent; nature: slightly warm. Indicated for brightening vision and calming fright; removes water-qi (edema). Taken long-term, lightens the body. The stem treats rashes with itching; can be decocted for a bath. Also called Yimu, Yiming, Dazha. Grows in ponds and marshes.

Muxiang (costus root). Taste: pungent; nature: warm. Indicated for pathogenic qi; wards off toxic pestilence and epidemic evil spirits; strengthens the will; treats strangury with turbid discharge. Taken long-term, prevents nightmares and sleep paralysis. Grows in mountain valleys.

Longdan (gentian). Taste: bitter, astringent. Indicated for cold-heat between the bones, fright epilepsy and pathogenic qi; mends severed injuries, stabilizes the five viscera, kills gu poison. Taken long-term, boosts intelligence and memory, lightens the body, and resists aging. Also called Lingyou. Grows in mountain valleys.

Tusizi (dodder seed). Taste: pungent; nature: neutral. Indicated for mending severed injuries, supplementing deficiency, boosting qi and strength, and promoting healthy weight. Its juice removes facial dark spots. Taken long-term, brightens vision, lightens the body, and prolongs life. Also called Tulu. Grows in mountain valleys.

Bajitian (Morinda root). Taste: pungent; nature: slightly warm. Indicated for severe wind pathogenic qi and impotence. Strengthens the sinews and bones, calms the five viscera, tonifies the middle, boosts the will, and benefits qi. Grows in mountain valleys.

Baimo. Taste: sweet; nature: cold. Indicated for cold-heat, the eight types of jaundice, and wasting-thirst. Tonifies the middle and benefits qi. Taken long-term, lightens the body and prolongs life. Also called Gucai.

Baihao (white mugwort). Taste: sweet; nature: neutral. Indicated for pathogenic qi of the five viscera, wind-cold-damp painful obstruction; tonifies the middle and benefits qi, makes the hair grow long and black, treats a hollow, hanging sensation in the heart region, and hunger despite little food. Taken long-term, lightens the body, sharpens hearing and vision, and prevents aging. Grows in river marshes.

Difuzi (kochia seed). Taste: bitter; nature: cold. Indicated for bladder heat; promotes urination, tonifies the middle and benefits essence-qi. Taken long-term, sharpens hearing and vision, lightens the body, and resists aging. Also called Dikui. Grows in level marshes.

Shilongchu (rush). Taste: bitter; nature: slightly cold. Indicated for pathogenic qi of the heart and abdomen, difficult urination, and urinary blockage; wind-damp, ghost-influx, and malign toxin. Taken long-term, tonifies deficiency and emaciation, lightens the body, sharpens hearing and vision, and prolongs life. Also called Longxu, Caoxuduan. Grows in mountain valleys.

Luoshi (climbing fig / Trachelospermum). Taste: bitter; nature: warm. Indicated for wind-heat with necrotic flesh, sores from carbuncles, dry mouth and scorched tongue, unresolving swollen carbuncles, swollen throat and tongue, and inability to swallow liquids. Taken long-term, lightens the body, brightens vision, moistens the skin and improves complexion, prevents aging and prolongs life. Also called Shiling. Grows in river valleys.

Huanglian (Coptis, goldthread). Taste: bitter; non-toxic. Indicated for heat qi, eye pain with damaged canthus and tearing; brightens vision, treats intestinal discharge with abdominal pain and diarrhea, and swelling and pain in women's genitalia. Taken long-term, improves memory. Also called Wanglian. Grows in river valleys. (Also grows attached to trees or walls.)

Wangbuliuxing (cowherb seed). Taste: bitter; nature: neutral. Indicated for wounds from blades; stops bleeding, expels pain, draws out embedded thorns, and removes wind painful obstruction and internal cold. Taken long-term, lightens the body, resists aging, and increases longevity. Grows in mountain valleys.

Lanshi (indigo plant seed). Taste: bitter; nature: cold. Indicated for resolving various poisons; kills parasites and dispels malign toxin from ghost-influx stings. Taken long-term, prevents the hair from graying, and lightens the body. Grows in level marshes. (The indigo scum from this plant is qingdai, indigo naturalis.)

Jingtian (orpine, Sedum). Taste: bitter, sour; nature: neutral. Indicated for severe heat and burns, bodily heat with vexation, and malign qi. Its flower treats women's red-and-white vaginal discharge, lightens the body, and brightens vision. Also called Jiehuo, Shenhuo. Grows in river valleys.

Tianmingjing (Carpesium). Taste: sweet; nature: cold. Indicated for stagnant blood and blood-mass conditions near death, bloody stool; stops bleeding, promotes urination, eliminates small parasites, removes painful obstruction, clears bound heat in the chest, and relieves vexing thirst. Taken long-term, lightens the body and resists aging. Also called Maijujiang, Xiamalan, Shishou. Grows in river marshes. (Also called Disong; its seed is called Heshi; its root is called Tuniuxi.)

Puhuang (cattail pollen). Taste: sweet; nature: neutral. Indicated for cold-heat of the heart, abdomen, and bladder; promotes urination, stops bleeding, and dissolves stagnant blood. Taken long-term, lightens the body, boosts qi and strength, and prolongs life like an immortal. Grows in ponds and marshes.

Xiangpu (cattail). Taste: sweet; nature: neutral. Indicated for pathogenic qi of the five viscera and below the heart, and foul rot in the mouth; firms the teeth, brightens vision, and sharpens hearing. Taken long-term, lightens the body and withstands fatigue. Also called [name illegible]. Grows in ponds and marshes.

Lancao (thoroughwort, Eupatorium). Taste: pungent; nature: neutral. Indicated for promoting the water passages; kills gu poison and wards off inauspicious influences. Taken long-term, benefits qi, lightens the body, prevents aging, and connects one with spirit intelligence. Also called Shuixiang. Grows in ponds and marshes.

Juemingzi (sicklepod seed). Taste: salty; nature: neutral. Indicated for blue blindness, excessive discharge from the eyes with a red-and-white membrane, and red, painful eyes with tearing. Taken long-term, benefits the essence and eyesight, and lightens the body. Grows in river marshes.

Yunshi (Caesalpinia seed). Taste: pungent; nature: slightly warm. Indicated for diarrhea and dysenteric discharge; kills parasites and gu poison, removes malign bound qi, stops pain, and eliminates cold-heat. Its flower treats hallucinations of seeing ghosts and spirits. Eating too much causes one to run about madly. Taken long-term, lightens the body and connects one with spirit intelligence. Grows in river valleys. (Also called Yuanshi, Tiandou, Yunying.)

Huangqi (Astragalus root). Taste: sweet; nature: slightly warm. Indicated for long-unhealed sores from carbuncles and abscesses; expels pus and stops pain, treats severe wind leprosy-like disease, the five types of hemorrhoids, and rat-fistula (scrofula); tonifies deficiency, and the hundred ailments of children. Also called Daishen. Grows in mountain valleys.

Shechuangzi (Cnidium seed). Taste: bitter; nature: neutral. Indicated for swelling and pain in women's genitalia, genital discharge in men, dampness and itching; removes painful obstruction qi, benefits the joints, and treats epilepsy and malignant sores. Taken long-term, lightens the body. Also called Shesu, Shemi. Grows in river valleys.

Loulu (Rhaponticum root). Taste: bitter, salty; nature: cold. Indicated for heat in the skin, malignant sores, carbuncles and hemorrhoids, and damp painful obstruction; promotes lactation. Taken long-term, lightens the body and benefits qi, sharpens hearing and vision, prevents aging, and prolongs life. Also called Yelan. Grows in mountain valleys.
Qiangen (madder root). Taste: bitter; nature: cold. Indicated for cold-damp wind painful obstruction and jaundice. Tonifies the middle. Grows in mountain valleys.

Xuanhua (bindweed flower). Taste: sweet; nature: warm. Indicated for benefiting qi and removing dark facial blemishes, making one attractive. Its root treats cold-heat pathogenic qi in the abdomen and promotes urination. Taken long-term, one does not hunger, and the body is lightened. Also called Jingenhua, Jinfei. Grows in level marshes.

Bai Tu Huo (White Rabbit Vine). Taste bitter, nature neutral. Indicated for venom of snakes and vipers, wasp and scorpion stings, mad-dog bites, and food poisoning; for ghost-possession (gu toxin, demonic infixation). Also called Bai Ge. Grows in mountain valleys.

Qing Rang (sesame sprout). Taste sweet, nature cold. Indicated for evil qi in the five viscera, wind-cold-damp bi syndrome; boosts qi, nourishes the brain and marrow, strengthens sinews and bones. Taken long-term, it sharpens hearing and vision, staves off hunger, prevents aging, and extends life. It is the sprout of the Ju Sheng plant (i.e., the sprout of sesame). Grows in river valleys.

Dang Gui (Chinese Angelica root). Taste sweet, nature warm, non-toxic. Indicated for cough with qi counterflow, warm malarial disorder with chills felt in the skin, women's abnormal uterine discharge and infertility, various malignant sores and ulcers, and blade wounds — taken as a decoction. Also called Gan Gui. Grows in river valleys.
Herbs — Middle Grade

Chi Jian (Gastrodia stalk). Taste pungent, nature warm. Kills ghost-essences and malign spirits; counters gu poison and malignant qi. Taken long-term, it boosts qi and strength, enhances yin vigor, makes one robust and healthy, lightens the body, and extends the years. Also called Li Mu and Gui Du You. Grows in river valleys.

Mai Men Dong (Ophiopogon tuber). Taste sweet, nature neutral. Indicated for bound qi in the heart and abdomen, damage from cold or from overeating, severance of the stomach's vessels and network vessels, emaciation and shortness of breath. Taken long-term, it lightens the body, prevents aging, and staves off hunger. Grows in river valleys.

Juan Bai (Selaginella / spikemoss). Taste pungent, nature neutral. Indicated for evil qi in the five viscera, cold-heat pain in a woman's genitals, concretions and masses, blood stasis blocking menstruation, and infertility. Taken long-term, it lightens the body and improves the complexion. Also called Wan Sui ("Ten Thousand Years"). Grows in mountain valleys.

Rou Cong Rong (Cistanche). Taste salty. Indicated for the five taxations and seven damages; tonifies the middle, relieves cold-heat pain in the penis, nourishes the five viscera, strengthens yin, boosts essence-qi, aids fertility, and treats women's concretions and masses. Taken long-term, it lightens the body. Grows in mountain valleys.

Ji Li Zi (Tribulus fruit / caltrop fruit). Taste bitter, nature warm. Indicated for malign blood; breaks up concretions, accumulations, and throat bi obstruction; treats difficult lactation. Taken long-term, it builds flesh and muscle, brightens the eyes, and lightens the body. Also called Pang Tong, Qu Ren, Zhi Xing, Chai Yu, and Sheng Tui. Grows in flat marshes.

Fang Feng (Saposhnikovia root). Taste sweet, nature warm, non-toxic. Indicated for severe wind-stroke with dizziness and pain, aversion to wind and wind-evil, blindness with no vision, wind traveling through the whole body, aching joints, and vexation with fullness. Taken long-term, it lightens the body. Also called Tong Yun. Grows in river marshes.

Sha Shen (Adenophora root). Taste bitter, nature slightly cold, non-toxic. Indicated for blood accumulation and fright qi; relieves cold-heat, tonifies the middle, and boosts lung qi. Taken long-term, it benefits the person. Also called Zhi Mu. Grows in river valleys.

Chuan Xiong (Ligusticum root). Taste pungent, nature warm, non-toxic. Indicated for wind-stroke entering the brain with headache, cold bi with sinew spasms and cramping, blade wounds, and women's blood-blocked menstruation with infertility. Grows in river valleys.

Mi Wu (Ligusticum leaf). Taste pungent, nature warm. Indicated for cough with qi counterflow; calms fright qi, wards off evil and malignancy, eliminates gu poison and ghost infixation, and expels the three worms. Taken long-term, it grants communion with the spirits. Also called Wei Wu.

Xu Duan (Dipsacus root, "Broken Continued"). Taste bitter, nature slightly warm. Indicated for cold-damage disease; tonifies deficiency, treats blade wounds, welling-abscesses, and injuries from falls and fractures; mends sinews and bones; treats women's difficult lactation. Taken long-term, it boosts qi and strength. Also called Long Dou and Shu Zhe. Grows in mountain valleys.

Yin Chen Hao (Virgate wormwood, Artemisia capillaris). Taste bitter, non-toxic. Indicated for wind-damp cold-heat evil qi, and heat-bind jaundice. Taken long-term, it lightens the body, boosts qi, and resists aging. Grows on Mount Tai and on hills and slopes.

Wu Wei Zi (Schisandra fruit). Taste sour, nature warm. Boosts qi; indicated for cough with qi counterflow, taxation damage with emaciation; tonifies deficiency, strengthens yin, and boosts a man's essence. Grows in mountain valleys.

Qin Jiao (Gentiana macrophylla root). Taste bitter, nature neutral. Indicated for cold-heat evil qi, cold-damp wind bi with limb and joint pain; drains water and disinhibits urination. Grows in mountain valleys.

Huang Qin (Scutellaria root). Taste bitter, nature neutral. Indicated for various heat-type jaundice, dysenteric diarrhea; expels water, breaks blocked menstruation, treats malign sores, eroding welling-abscesses, and fire burns. Also called Fu Chang. Grows in river valleys.

Shao Yao (Peony root). Taste bitter. Indicated for evil-qi abdominal pain; relieves blood bi, breaks up hard accumulations and cold-heat hernia-like masses, stops pain, disinhibits urination, and boosts qi. Grows in river valleys.

Gan Jiang (dried ginger). Taste pungent, nature warm. Indicated for chest fullness with cough and qi counterflow; warms the middle, stops bleeding, induces sweating, and expels wind-damp bi; treats dysenteric diarrhea — the fresh root is especially good. Taken long-term, it removes foul odor from the body and grants clarity of spirit. Grows in river valleys.

Gao Ben (Ligusticum sinense). Taste pungent, nature slightly warm. Indicated for women's hernia-like masses, cold swelling and pain of the genitals, urgency and cramping in the abdomen; relieves wind headache, builds the flesh, and beautifies the complexion. Also called Gui Qing and Di Xin. Grows in mountain valleys.

Ma Huang (Ephedra). Taste bitter, nature warm, non-toxic. Indicated for wind-stroke and cold-damage headache, warm malarial disorder; releases the exterior and induces sweating, removes evil heat qi, stops cough with qi counterflow, relieves cold-heat, and breaks up firm concretions and accumulations. Also called Long Sha. Grows in mountain valleys.

Ge Gen (Kudzu root). Taste sweet, nature neutral. Indicated for wasting-thirst, severe body heat, vomiting, and various bi syndromes; raises yin qi and resolves various poisons. The kudzu seed (Ge Gu) treats chronic diarrhea of more than ten years' standing. Also called Ji Qi Gen. Grows in river valleys.

Zhi Mu (Anemarrhena rhizome). Taste bitter, nature cold, non-toxic. Indicated for wasting-thirst with heat in the middle; removes evil qi, treats limb edema, drains water, tonifies deficiency, and boosts qi. Also called Mu Chi, Lian Mu, Ye Liao, Di Shen, Shui Shen, Shui Jun, and Huo Mu. Grows in river valleys.

Bei Mu (Fritillaria bulb). Taste pungent, nature neutral. Indicated for cold-damage disease with vexing heat, dribbling urinary difficulty, evil qi, hernia-like masses, throat bi, difficult lactation, blade wounds, and wind tetany. Also called Kong Cao.

Gua Lou (Trichosanthes fruit). Taste bitter, nature cold. Indicated for wasting-thirst, body heat with vexation and fullness, and severe heat; tonifies deficiency, calms the middle, and mends severed injuries. Also called Di Lou. Grows in river valleys and on shaded mountainsides.

Dan Shen (Salvia miltiorrhiza root). Taste bitter, nature slightly cold, non-toxic. Indicated for evil qi in the heart and abdomen, faint gurgling intestinal sounds like running water, cold-heat accumulations; breaks up concretions and eliminates masses, stops vexing fullness, and boosts qi. Also called Que Chan Cao. Grows in mountain valleys.

Xuanshen (Scrophularia root). Taste bitter, slightly cold, non-toxic. Indicated for cold-heat accumulations in the abdomen, and women's residual ailments after childbirth or nursing. Tonifies kidney qi and brightens the eyes. Also called Chongtai. Grows in river valleys.

Kushen (Sophora flavescens root). Taste bitter, cold. Indicated for bound qi in the heart and abdomen, hernia-like masses and accumulations, jaundice, dribbling residual urination; expels water, removes carbuncles and swelling. Tonifies the middle, brightens the eyes, stops tearing. Also called Shuihuai, also called Kushi. Grows in mountain valleys and fields.

Gouji ("dog spine" root, Cibotium rhizome). Taste bitter, neutral. Indicated for stiffness of the lower back, slackness or spasm of the joints, generalized painful obstruction (bi), and cold-damp knee pain. Quite beneficial for the elderly. Also called Baizhi. Grows in river valleys.

Bixie (Dioscorea root). Taste bitter, neutral. Indicated for stiff pain of the lower back, and wind-cold-damp painful obstruction (bi) of the bones and joints. For malignant sores that will not heal, and heat qi. Grows in mountain valleys.

Tongcao (Akebia/Tetrapanax stem). Taste pungent, neutral. Expels harmful parasites, removes cold-heat of the spleen and stomach, frees the nine orifices, the blood vessels, and the joints, and improves memory. Also called Fuzhi. Grows in mountain valleys.

Qumai (Dianthus, fringed pink). Taste bitter, cold. Indicated for obstruction and blockage disorders of all kinds, urinary stoppage; expels splinters, resolves carbuncles and swelling, brightens the eyes and removes cataracts, breaks a stagnant pregnancy to induce miscarriage, and discharges blocked blood. Also called Jujumai. Grows in river valleys.

Baijiang (Patrinia root). Taste bitter, neutral. Indicated for sudden fiery heat sores, red qi, scabies and itching, ulcers and hemorrhoids, saddle sores, and heat qi. Also called Luchang. Grows in river valleys.

Baizhi (Angelica dahurica root). Taste pungent, warm. Indicated for women's red-and-white vaginal discharge, blood blockage, genital swelling, cold-heat, wind headache, and eye afflictions with tearing; promotes growth and moistening of flesh and skin, and can be made into a facial ointment. Also called Fangxiang. Grows in river valleys.

Duruo (Pollia japonica). Taste pungent, slightly warm. Indicated for rebellious qi below the chest and ribs; warms the middle, treats wind entering the cranial cavity with head swelling and pain, and excessive nasal mucus and tearing. Taken long-term, boosts the essence, brightens the eyes, and lightens the body. Also called Duheng. Grows in river marshes.

Zicao (Lithospermum, purple gromwell root). Taste bitter, cold. Indicated for evil qi of the heart and abdomen, and the five types of jaundice; tonifies the middle and boosts qi, frees the nine orifices, and opens the waterways. Also called Zidan, also called Zifu, also called Dixue. Grows in mountain valleys.

Ziwan (Aster tataricus root). Taste bitter, warm. Indicated for cough with rebellious ascending qi, and bound cold-heat qi in the chest; removes gu-poison toxin and wilting paralysis, and calms the five viscera. Grows in mountain valleys.

Baixian (Dictamnus dasycarpus, dittany bark). Taste bitter, cold. Indicated for head wind, jaundice, cough with rebellious qi, dribbling urinary difficulty, women's genital swelling and pain, and damp painful obstruction (bi) with numb, dead flesh where one cannot bend or stretch the limbs, or rise and walk. Grows in river valleys.

Weixian. Taste bitter, neutral. Indicated for wind-damp painful obstruction (bi), joint pain throughout the body, fright epilepsy with tongue protrusion, palpitations from malicious wind, and rat-fistula (scrofulous) swellings. Also called Mixian. Grows in river marshes.

Baiwei (Cynanchum atratum root). Taste bitter, neutral. Indicated for sudden wind-stroke with body heat, fullness of the limbs, confusion where one fails to recognize others, mania and delusion from evil qi, cold-heat with aching pain, and warm malaria with shivering chills that recur at fixed times. Grows in river marshes.

Xier fruit (Xanthium, cocklebur fruit). Taste sweet, warm. Indicated for wind-head cold pain, wind-damp generalized painful obstruction (bi), cramping pain of the four limbs, and malignant, dead flesh. Taken long-term, boosts qi, sharpens the ears and eyes, strengthens resolve, and lightens the body. Also called Huxi, also called Digui. Grows in river valleys.

Maogen (Imperata cylindrica root, cogongrass root). Taste sweet, cold. Indicated for taxation damage and vacuity emaciation; tonifies the middle and boosts qi, removes stasis blood, and treats blood blockage with cold-heat, and frees urination. Its shoot treats water swelling. Also called Langen, also called Rugen. Grows in mountain valleys.

Suanjiang (Physalis, ground cherry). Taste sour, neutral. Indicated for heat vexation and fullness; calms the will and boosts qi, frees urination, and for difficult labor — swallowing its fruit brings immediate delivery. Also called Cujiang. Grows in river marshes.

Yinyanghuo (Epimedium, horny goat weed). Taste pungent, cold. Indicated for impotence and injury with loss of essence, pain in the penis; frees urination, boosts qi and strength, and strengthens resolve. Also called Gangqian. Grows in mountain valleys.

Lishi (Iris lactea seed). Taste sweet, neutral. Indicated for cold-heat of the skin, heat qi in the stomach, and wind-cold-damp painful obstruction (bi); strengthens the sinews and bones and increases appetite. Taken long-term, lightens the body. The flowers and leaves expel white parasites. Also called Jucao, also called Sanjian, also called Shishou. Grows in river valleys.

Kuandong (Tussilago farfara, coltsfoot flower). Taste pungent, warm. Indicated for cough with rebellious ascending qi, tendency to wheeze, throat obstruction, various types of fright epilepsy, and cold-heat evil qi. Also called Tuowu, Kedong, Huxu, and Tuxi. Grows in mountain valleys.

Fangji (Stephania tetrandra root). Taste pungent, neutral. Indicated for wind-cold, warm malaria, and heat qi, and various types of epilepsy; removes evil qi and frees urination and defecation. Also called Jieli. Grows in river valleys.

Nüwan. Taste pungent, warm. Indicated for wind-cold with shivering chills, cholera-like sudden turmoil with diarrhea and dysentery, intestinal rumbling that moves erratically up and down, fright epilepsy, cold-heat, and a hundred other ailments. Grows in river valleys.

Zelan (Lycopus lucidus, bugleweed). Taste sour, non-toxic. Indicated for nosebleed in nursing women, residual ailments after wind-stroke, great abdominal water swelling, edema of the body, face, and four limbs, water lodged in the joints, wounds from blades, and carbuncular swelling with suppurating sores. Also called Hulan, also called Longzao. Grows beside marshes.

Diyu (Sanguisorba officinalis, burnet root). Taste bitter, slightly cold. Indicated for women's breast pain, the seven damages, and vaginal discharge disease; stops pain, removes malignant flesh, stops sweating, and treats wounds from blades. Grows in mountain valleys.

Wangsun. Taste bitter, neutral, non-toxic. Indicated for evil qi of the five viscera, cold-damp painful obstruction (bi), aching pain of the four limbs, and cold, painful knees. Grows in river valleys.

Juechuang (Justicia procumbens). Taste salty, cold. Indicated for pain of the lower back and spine so severe one cannot lie on a bed, and difficulty bending and straightening; removes heat, and can be used as a bathing decoction. Grows in mountain valleys.

Ma Xian Hao (Pedicularis). Taste bitter, nature neutral. Indicated for cold-heat ghost-attack fevers, wind-stroke with damp painful obstruction, women's vaginal discharge disorders, and infertility. Also called Ma Shi Hao. Grows in river marshes.

Shu Yang Quan (a Solanum species). Taste bitter, nature slightly cold. Indicated for bald scalp with malignant sores, heat-qi scabies and itching with crusted lichen and worms; also cures dental caries. Grows in mountain valleys.
Ji Xue Cao (Centella asiatica, gotu kola). Taste bitter, nature cold. Indicated for severe heat, malignant sores and welling-abscesses, spreading erysipelas with redness, red skin, and fever.

Yuan Yi (wall lichen/moss). Taste sour, non-toxic. Indicated for jaundice, vexation with cough and counterflow qi, blood-qi disorders, sudden heat lodged in the intestines and stomach, and internal cold from wounds by blades. Taken long-term, it tonifies the middle, boosts qi, builds muscle, and improves complexion. Also called Xi Xie, Wu Jiu, Yuan Ying, Tian Jiu, and Shu Jiu. Grows in the shade of old walls or on rooftops.

Shui Ping (duckweed). Taste pungent, nature cold. Indicated for sudden heat with itchy body; moves water qi downward, counteracts the effects of alcohol, lengthens beard and hair, and stops wasting-thirst. Taken long-term, it lightens the body. Also called Shui Hua. Grows in ponds and marshes.

Hai Zao (seaweed). Taste bitter, nature cold. Indicated for goiter and swelling qi, nodules below the neck; breaks up and disperses bound qi, welling-abscess swellings, hardened concretions and conglomerations, rumbling in the abdomen, and reduces the twelve types of water swelling. Also called Luo Shou. Grows in ponds and marshes.

Jie Geng (Platycodon, balloon flower). Taste bitter, non-toxic. Indicated for chest and rib-side pain like a knife stabbing, abdominal fullness with faint rumbling, and fright with palpitating qi. Grows in mountain valleys.

Xuan Fu Hua (Inula flower). Taste salty, nature warm. Indicated for bound qi, fullness below the ribs, and fright palpitations; eliminates water and removes cold-heat between the five viscera. Tonifies the middle and moves qi downward. Also called Jin Fei Cao and Sheng Zhan. Grows in river valleys.

She Xian. Taste bitter, nature slightly cold. Indicated for fright epilepsy with cold-heat evil qi; eliminates heat, treats wounds from blades, welling-abscesses, hemorrhoids, rat-fistula (scrofula), malignant sores, and head ulcers. Also called She Xian ("snake-holder"). Grows in mountain valleys.

Jia Su (Schizonepeta). Taste pungent, nature warm. Indicated for cold-heat, rat-fistula, scrofula sores; breaks up bound and accumulated qi, moves stagnant blood downward, and eliminates damp painful obstruction. Also called Shu Ming. Grows in river marshes.
Herbs — Lower Grade

Ying Shi (wild rose hip). Taste sour, nature warm. Indicated for welling-abscesses and malignant sores, knotted flesh and injured sinews, festering sores with heat qi, and non-healing yin erosion; benefits the joints. Also called Qiang Wei, Qiang Ma, and Niu Ji. Grows in mountain valleys.

Mu Dan (tree peony bark). Taste pungent, nature cold. Indicated for cold-heat and wind-stroke, convulsions and tetany, fright epilepsy with evil qi; eliminates hard concretions and stagnant blood lodged in the intestines and stomach, calms the five viscera, and treats welling-abscess sores. Also called Lu Jiu and Shu Gu. Grows in mountain valleys.

Shi Wei (Pyrrosia, felt fern). Taste bitter, nature neutral. Indicated for taxation heat and evil qi, the five types of blocked, painful urination; benefits urination and the water passages. Also called Shi [character illegible] Zhe. Grows on rocks in mountain valleys.

Bai He (lily bulb). Taste sweet, nature neutral. Indicated for evil qi with abdominal distension and heart pain; benefits urination and defecation. Tonifies the middle and boosts qi. Grows in river valleys.

Zi Shen. Taste bitter, nature cold. Indicated for accumulations and masses in the heart and abdomen, cold-heat evil qi; opens the nine orifices and benefits urination and defecation. Also called Mu Meng. Grows in mountain valleys.

Wang Gua (Trichosanthes cucumeroides). Taste bitter, nature cold. Indicated for wasting-thirst with internal painful obstruction, stagnant blood with amenorrhea, cold-heat with aching pain; boosts qi and cures deafness. Also called Tu Gua. Grows in flat marshes.

Da Huang (rhubarb). Taste bitter, nature cold, toxic. Governs the draining of stagnant blood and blood block, cold-heat, and breaks up concretions, conglomerations, accumulations, and masses, as well as lingering fluids and old food; flushes and cleanses the intestines and stomach, expels the old to bring in the new, frees the passage of water and grain, regulates the middle and aids digestion, and calms and harmonizes the five viscera. Grows in mountain valleys.

Gan Sui (Euphorbia kansui). Taste bitter, nature cold, toxic. Indicated for large-abdomen mounting-conglomerations, abdominal fullness, swelling of the face and eyes, and lingering fluids and old food; breaks up hard concretions, accumulations, and masses, and benefits the passage of water and grain. Also called Zhu Tian. Grows in river valleys.

Ting Li (pepperweed seed). Taste pungent, nature cold. Indicated for concretions, conglomerations, accumulations, and masses with bound qi, and cold-heat from food and drink; breaks up hardness, expels evil qi, and frees the water passages. Also called Da Shi and Da Shi. Grows in flat marshes and fields.

Yuan Hua (Genkwa flower). Taste bitter, nature warm, toxic. Indicated for cough with counterflow ascending qi, wheezing with throat rattling, swollen throat with shortness of breath, gu-poisoning and ghost malaria, mounting-conglomerations and welling-abscess swellings; kills insects and fish. Also called Qu Shui. Grows in river valleys.

Ze Qi (Euphorbia helioscopia). Taste bitter, nature slightly cold. Indicated for skin heat, large-abdomen water qi, swelling of the four limbs, face, and eyes, and insufficient yin qi in men. Grows in river marshes.

Da Ji (Euphorbia pekinensis). Taste bitter, nature cold. Indicated for gu-poisoning and the twelve types of water swelling, abdominal fullness with acute pain, accumulations and masses, wind-stroke, skin pain, and vomiting with counterflow. Also called Mao Ju.

Rao Hua (Wikstroemia flower). Taste bitter, nature cold. Indicated for cold damage and warm malaria; drains the twelve types of water, breaks up accumulations, masses, and great hard concretions, flushes and cleanses lingering stagnation and old food in the intestines and stomach, cold-heat evil qi, and benefits the water passages. Grows in river valleys.

Gou Wen (Gelsemium elegans). Taste pungent, nature warm. Indicated for wounds from blades and breast ailments, contracted malign wind, cough with counterflow ascending qi, and water swelling; kills ghost-attack and gu-poison. Also called Zhi Ge. Grows in mountain valleys.

Li Lu (Veratrum, false hellebore). Taste pungent, nature cold, toxic. Indicated for gu-poisoning, cough with counterflow, diarrhea and dysentery with intestinal flushing, head ulcers, scabies, itching, and malignant sores; kills all manner of worm toxins and removes dead flesh. Also called Huang Ran. Grows in mountain valleys.

Wu Tou (Aconitum, aconite). Taste pungent, nature warm, toxic. Indicated for wind-stroke and malign wind with shivering chills; induces sweating, eliminates cold-damp painful obstruction, cough with counterflow ascending qi, and breaks up accumulations, masses, and cold-heat. Its decocted juice is called She Wang, used to kill birds and beasts. Also called Xi Du, Ji Zi, and Wu Hui. Grows in mountain valleys.

Tianxiong ("Heavenly Hero", aconite). Taste: pungent. Nature: warm. Indicated for severe wind, cold-damp bi syndrome, joint pain, spasms and contractures, breaks up masses and accumulations, evil qi, wounds from blades; strengthens sinews and bones, lightens the body and eases walking. Also called Baimu. Grows in mountain valleys.

Fuzi (aconite root). Taste: pungent. Nature: warm. Toxic. Indicated for wind-cold cough with rising qi, evil qi; warms the middle, treats wounds from blades, breaks up hard masses and accumulations, blood conglomerations and cold-damp, sprains and spasms, knee pain that prevents walking. Grows in mountain valleys.

Yang zhizhu (Rhododendron molle). Taste: pungent. Nature: warm. Toxic. Indicated for invasive wind lingering and creeping with pain in the skin, warm malaria, malignant toxin, and various bi syndromes. Grows in river valleys.

Yinyu (Skimmia). Taste: bitter. Nature: warm. Indicated for evil qi in the five viscera, cold-heat and emaciation of the heart and abdomen occurring at set times like malaria, and wind-damp bi pain in the joints. Grows in river valleys.

Shegan (Belamcanda / blackberry lily). Taste: bitter. Nature: neutral. Indicated for cough with rising qi, throat bi with pain and difficulty breathing; disperses bound qi, treats evil counterflow in the abdomen and great heat from food and drink. Also called Wushan, also called Wupu. Grows in river valleys.

Yuanwei (Iris). Taste: bitter. Nature: neutral. Indicated for gu poison and evil qi, ghost-influx disorders and various poisons; breaks up masses and accumulations, expels water, and drives out the three worms. Grows in mountain valleys.

Guanzhong (Dryopteris rhizome). Taste: bitter. Nature: slightly cold. Toxic. Indicated for evil heat qi in the abdomen and various poisons; kills the three worms. Also called Guanjie, Guanqu, Baitou, Hujuan, and Bianfu. Grows in mountain valleys.

Feilian (thistle). Taste: bitter. Nature: neutral. Indicated for heat in the bones and joints, heaviness and aching pain in the shins. Taken long-term, it lightens the body. Also called Feiqing. Grows in river marshes.

Banxia (Pinellia ternata). Taste: pungent. Nature: neutral. Indicated for cold damage with alternating cold and heat, hardness below the heart; directs qi downward, treats swelling and pain of the throat, dizziness, chest distension, cough with rising qi, and intestinal rumbling; stops sweating. Also called Diwen, also called Shuiyu. Grows in river valleys.

Huzhang ("Tiger's Palm"). Taste: bitter. Nature: warm. Non-toxic. Indicated for heart pain, cold-heat, bound qi, accumulations and the "hidden beam" mass; treats injury to the sinews with wilting and spasm, and disinhibits the water passages. Grows in mountain valleys.

Langdangzi (henbane seed). Taste: bitter. Nature: cold. Indicated for toothache, expels worms, treats flesh bi with spasm and tension; makes one walk vigorously and see visions of ghosts. Eaten in excess, it makes one run about madly. Taken long-term, it lightens the body, letting one run to keep pace with a galloping horse, strengthens the will, boosts strength, and opens communication with spirits. Also called Hengtang. Grows in river valleys.

Shuqi (young shoots of Dichroa febrifuga). Taste: pungent. Nature: neutral. Toxic. Indicated for malaria and cough with rising qi and cold-heat, hard masses and binding in the abdomen, bound accumulations of evil qi, gu poison, and ghost-influx. Grows in river valleys.

Hengshan (Dichroa febrifuga root). Taste: bitter. Nature: cold. Indicated for cold damage with cold-heat, heat outbreaks and warm malaria, ghost poison, phlegm binding in the chest, and vomiting with counterflow. Also called Hucao. Grows in river valleys.

Qingxiang (Celosia). Taste: bitter. Nature: slightly cold. Indicated for evil qi, heat in the skin, wind itch and body itching; kills the three worms. Its seeds, called Caojueming, treat darkened lips and mouth. Also called Caohao, also called Louhao. Grows in level valleys.

Langya ("Wolf's Tooth"). Taste: bitter. Nature: cold. Toxic. Indicated for evil qi and heat qi, scabies and itching, malignant sores and hemorrhoids; expels white worms. Also called Yazi. Grows in river valleys.

Bailian (Ampelopsis japonica). Taste: bitter. Nature: neutral. Indicated for carbuncles, swelling, and deep-rooted sores; disperses bound qi, stops pain and clears heat, treats redness in the eyes, infantile fright convulsions, warm malaria, and swelling and pain in women's genitals. Also called Tuhe, also called Baicao. Grows in mountain valleys.

Baiji (Bletilla striata). Taste: bitter. Nature: neutral. Indicated for carbuncles, swelling, malignant and festering sores, injury to yin, dead flesh, evil qi in the stomach, invasive wind and "ghost strike" (sudden collapse), and flaccidity with loss of control of the limbs. Also called Gangen, also called Lianjicao. Grows in river valleys.

Caohao (Artemisia annua, sweet wormwood). Taste: bitter. Nature: cold. Indicated for scabies, itching, and malignant sores; kills lice, clears heat lingering in the joints, and brightens the eyes. Also called Qinghao, also called Fangkui. Grows in river marshes.

Guanjun (a reed-root fungus). Taste: salty. Nature: neutral. Indicated for heart pain; warms the middle, expels long worms, baixi-worms, and pinworms, treats snakebite poison, and masses with various worms. Also called Guanlu. Grows in ponds and marshes.

Lianqiao (Forsythia suspensa). Taste: bitter. Nature: neutral. Indicated for cold-heat and scrofulous fistulas, scrofula, consumption, carbuncles and swelling, malignant sores, goiter and tumors, and bound heat with gu poison. Also called Yiqiao, Jianhua, Zhegen, and Sanlian. Grows in mountain valleys.

Baitougong (Pulsatilla chinensis). Taste: bitter. Nature: warm. Non-toxic. Indicated for warm malaria with mania and personality change, cold-heat, masses and accumulations, and goiter; expels stagnant blood, stops pain, and treats wounds from blades. Also called Yezhangren, also called Huwang Shizhe. Grows in river valleys.

Lüru. Taste: sour and salty. Toxic. Treats malignant necrotic flesh, festering sores, and dead flesh; kills scabies mites, expels pus and bad blood, eliminates severe wind and heat qi, and treats forgetfulness and unhappiness. Grows in river valleys.

Yangtao. Taste: bitter. Nature: cold. Indicated for scorching heat with sudden reddening of the body, wind-water accumulations, and malignant sores; eliminates heat in children. Also called Guitao, also called Yangchang. Grows in river valleys.

Yangti (Rumex japonicus, dock). Taste: bitter. Nature: cold. Indicated for scalp baldness and scabies with itching; eliminates heat and treats erosive sores in women's genitals. Also called Dongfangsu, Lianchonglu, and Guimu. Grows in river marshes.

Lufu (wild soybean). Taste: bitter. Nature: neutral. Indicated for gu poison, women's lower back and abdominal pain with unhappiness, intestinal abscess, scrofula, consumption, and sores. Grows in mountain valleys. — Niubian. Taste: bitter. Nature: slightly cold. Indicated for sores and heat qi in the skin; can be made into a bath decoction, kills cattle lice and small parasites, and also treats cattle diseases. Grows in river valleys.

Luying (Sambucus, elder). Taste: bitter. Nature: cold. Indicated for various bi syndromes between the bones, spasms and aching pain of the four limbs, cold pain in the knees, impotence, shortness of breath with insufficiency, and swelling of the feet. Grows in river valleys.

Jin Cao (Arthraxon). Taste bitter, nature neutral. Indicated for chronic cough with rising qi, panting and counterflow from prolonged cold, fright palpitations, scabby itch, white bald patches (tinea), and sores; kills small parasites on the skin. Grows in river valleys.

Xia Ku Cao (self-heal, Prunella vulgaris). Taste bitter and pungent, nature cold. Indicated for cold-heat scrofula and rat-fistula sores of the head, breaks up concretions, disperses goiter and knotted qi, and treats swollen legs from damp bi. Lightens the body. Also called Xi Ju and Nai Dong. Grows in river valleys.
Wu Jiu (a black fern). Taste sweet, nature cold. Indicated for skin ailments with alternating cold and heat; benefits the qi of the small intestine and bladder. Grows in mountain valleys.

Zao Xiu (Paris polyphylla). Taste bitter, nature slightly cold. Indicated for fright epilepsy with head-shaking and tongue-lolling, heat qi lodged in the abdomen, madness, welling sores with yin-region erosion; expels the three kinds of intestinal parasites and removes snake venom. Also called Shi Xiu. Grows in river valleys.

Shi Chang Sheng. Taste salty, nature slightly cold. Indicated for cold-heat malign sores and severe heat; wards off ghost qi and inauspicious influences. Also called Dan Cao. Grows in mountain valleys.

Lang Du (wolfsbane). Taste pungent, nature neutral. Indicated for cough with counterflow qi, breaks up accumulations and food stagnation, cold-heat water-qi disorders, malign sores, rat-fistula and gangrenous erosion, ghost-essence and gu poison; kills birds and beasts. Also called Xu Du. Grows in mountain valleys.

Gui Jiu (Dysosma). Taste pungent, nature warm. Governs the killing of gu poison and ghost-visitation essence-things, wards off malign qi and inauspicious influences, expels evil, and resolves the hundred poisons. Also called Jue Xi, Ma Mu Du Gong, and Jiu Jiu. Grows in mountain valleys.

Bian Xu (knotweed, Polygonum aviculare). Taste bitter, nature neutral. Indicated for spreading scabby itch and fistulous sores or hemorrhoids; kills the three kinds of intestinal parasites. Grows in mountain valleys.

Shang Lu (pokeweed, Phytolacca). Taste pungent, nature neutral. Indicated for water distension and hernia-like conglomeration bi; applied as a hot compress it removes swellings and sores. Kills ghost-essence things. Also called [illegible]-gen and Ye Hu. Grows in river valleys.

Nu Qing. Taste pungent, nature neutral. Indicated for gu poison, expels evil malign qi, kills ghosts and warm-malaria, and wards off inauspicious influences. Also called Que Piao. Grows in mountain valleys.

Bai Fu Zi (Typhonium). Indicated for heart pain and blood bi, and the hundred ailments of the face; it also enhances the potency of other drugs. Grows in Shu commandery.

Gu Huo. Taste sweet, nature warm. Indicated for severe wind evil qi and damp bi with cold pain. Taken long-term, lightens the body, prolongs life, and resists aging. Also called Dong Kui Zi. Grows in river marshes.
Bie Ji. Taste bitter, nature slightly warm. Indicated for wind-cold-damp bi, heaviness of the body, aching and cold in the four limbs, and evil qi causing joint pain. Grows in river valleys.

Shi Xia Chang Qing (Cynanchum paniculatum). Taste salty, nature neutral. Indicated for ghost-visitation essence-things and evil malign qi; kills the hundred essence-spirits and gu poison, old specters and shifting visitations, aimless wandering and wailing, sorrow and confusion. Also called Xu Chang Qing. Grows in ponds and marshes.

Qiao Gen (Forsythia root). Taste bitter, nature cold. Indicated for draining heat qi from the lower body, boosts yin essence, gives the face an attractive glow, and brightens the eyes. Taken long-term, lightens the body and resists aging. Grows in level marshes.
Trees and Wood — Superior Grade

Fu Ling (Poria). Taste sweet, nature neutral. Indicated for counterflow qi in the chest and rib-sides, worry, anger, fright, and fear, knotted pain below the heart, cold-heat with vexation, fullness, cough and counterflow qi, dry mouth and dry tongue; promotes urination. Taken long-term, calms the spirit and nourishes the mind, staves off hunger, and prolongs life. Also called Fu Tu. Grows in mountain valleys.

Pine resin. Taste bitter, nature warm. Indicated for welling abscesses and malign sores, head sores and white bald patches, scabby itch and wind ailments; calms the five viscera and clears heat. Taken long-term, lightens the body and prolongs life. Also called Song Gao and Song Fang. Grows in mountain valleys.

Bai Shi (arborvitae seed, Biota). Taste sweet, nature neutral. Indicated for fright palpitations; calms the five viscera, boosts qi, and removes wind-damp bi. Taken long-term, gives one a moist and beautiful complexion, sharpens the ears and eyes, staves off hunger and aging, lightens the body, and prolongs life. Grows in mountain valleys.

Jun Gui (a fine cinnamon bark). Taste pungent, nature warm. Indicated for the hundred ailments; nourishes the essence-spirit, harmonizes the complexion, and serves as the guiding envoy for other drugs. Taken long-term, lightens the body, resists aging, gives the face a radiant glow, and keeps one as charming as a child. Grows in the mountain valleys of Jiaozhi.

Mu Gui (cinnamon bark). Taste pungent, nature warm. Indicated for rising cough with counterflow qi, knotted qi, throat bi with retching on inhalation; benefits the joints and supplements the middle to boost qi. Taken long-term, connects one with the spirit, lightens the body, and resists aging. Grows in mountain valleys.

Du Zhong (Eucommia). Taste pungent, nature neutral. Indicated for lower back and knee pain; supplements the middle, boosts essence qi, strengthens sinews and bones, fortifies the will, relieves itching and dampness below the genitals, and dribbling after urination. Taken long-term, lightens the body and resists aging. Also called Si Xian. Grows in mountain valleys.

Man Jing Shi (Vitex fruit). Taste bitter, nature slightly cold. Indicated for cold-heat between the sinews and bones, damp bi with spasm and contracture; brightens the eyes, strengthens the teeth, benefits the nine orifices, and expels white parasites. Taken long-term, lightens the body and resists aging. The small variety of vitex fruit has equal effect.

Bo Mu (Phellodendron bark). Taste bitter, nature cold. Indicated for knotted heat qi in the five viscera and the intestines and stomach, jaundice, intestinal hemorrhoids; stops diarrhea and dysentery, women's red-and-white vaginal discharge, and erosive sores of the genitals. The root is also called Tan Huan. Grows in mountain valleys.

Xin Yi (magnolia flower bud). Taste pungent, nature warm. Indicated for cold-heat of the five viscera and body, wind-induced head and brain pain, and dark blemishes on the face. Taken long-term, lowers qi, lightens the body, brightens the eyes, and extends one's years into old age. Also called Xin Yin, Hou Tao, and Fang Mu. Grows in river valleys.

Yu Pi (elm bark). Taste sweet, nature neutral. Indicated for stoppage of urination and defecation; promotes the passage of water and removes evil qi. Taken long-term, lightens the body and staves off hunger. The fruit (elm samara) is even more effective. Also called Ling Yu. Grows in mountain valleys.

Suan Zao (sour jujube). Taste sour, nature neutral. Indicated for cold-heat and evil knotted qi in the heart and abdomen, aching in the four limbs, and damp bi. Taken long-term, calms the five viscera, lightens the body, and prolongs life. Grows in river marshes.

Huai Zi (Sophora seed). Taste bitter, nature cold. Indicated for evil heat qi within the five viscera; stops drooling and excess saliva, mends internal injuries, treats the five kinds of hemorrhoids and fire sores, women's breast concretions, and painful spasm of the uterus. Grows in level marshes.

Gouqi (Lycium / wolfberry). Taste bitter, cold nature. Indicated for evil qi within the five viscera, heat in the middle with wasting-thirst, and bi-syndrome (painful obstruction) throughout the body. Taken long-term, strengthens sinews and bones, lightens the body, and delays aging. Also called Qigen, Digu, Goujii, Difu. Grows in marshy lowlands.

Juyou (tangerine/pomelo peel). Taste pungent, warm. Indicated for concretions and heat with rebellious qi in the chest; promotes the free flow of water and grain (digestion). Taken long-term, removes bad breath, lowers qi, and clears the spirit. Also called Jupi (tangerine peel). Grows in the mountain valleys of the south.
Trees and Wood — Middle Grade

Ganqi (dried lacquer). Taste pungent, warm; non-toxic. Indicated for severe injury; supplements the middle, mends sinews and bones, fills the marrow and brain, calms the five viscera, and treats the five slacknesses and six urgencies, and wind-cold-damp bi. Fresh lacquer kills long worms (intestinal parasites). Taken long-term, lightens the body and delays aging. Grows in river valleys.

Mulan (magnolia). Taste bitter, cold. Indicated for severe heat within the skin; removes facial heat, redness, pustules and rosacea, aversion to wind, leprosy-like sores, and itching dampness beneath the genitals; brightens the eyes. Also called Linlan. Grows in mountain valleys.

Nüzhenshi (glossy privet fruit, Ligustrum lucidum). Taste bitter, neutral. Mainly supplements the middle, calms the five viscera, nourishes essence and spirit, and removes the hundred diseases. Taken long-term, makes one plump and healthy, lightens the body, and prevents aging. Grows in river valleys.

Sangshang jisheng (mulberry mistletoe). Taste bitter, neutral. Indicated for lower back pain, stiff back in children, welling-abscesses and swelling; secures the fetus, fills out the flesh, strengthens teeth and hair, and lengthens the beard and eyebrows. Its fruit brightens the eyes, lightens the body, and clears the spirit. Also called Jixie, Yumu, Wantong. Grows in mountain valleys.

Ruihe (蕤核, Prinsepia seed). Taste sweet, neutral; non-toxic. Indicated for knotted evil qi in the heart and abdomen; brightens the eyes, and treats red, painful eyes with tearing. Taken long-term, lightens the body, boosts qi, and staves off hunger. Grows in river valleys.

Longyan (longan). Taste sweet, neutral. Indicated for evil qi in the five viscera; calms the will and relieves loss of appetite. Taken long-term, strengthens the ethereal soul (hun), sharpens the mind, lightens the body, prevents aging, and clears the spirit. Grows in mountain valleys.

Houpo (magnolia bark). Taste bitter, warm; non-toxic. Indicated for wind-stroke, cold damage (shanghan), headache, cold-heat with fright qi, and blood bi with numb, dead flesh; expels the three worms. Grows in mountain valleys.

Zhuye (bamboo leaf). Taste bitter, neutral. Indicated for cough with rebellious ascending qi, overflowing sinew spasms, and malign sores; kills small worms. The root, made into a decoction, boosts qi, quenches thirst, supplements deficiency, and lowers qi. Its juice treats wind convulsions and bi. The fruit clears the spirit, lightens the body, and boosts qi.

Zhishi (bitter orange, immature fruit). Taste bitter, cold. Indicated for severe wind in the skin causing itching like hemp seeds or beans; removes cold-heat and heat binding, and stops dysentery. Builds up flesh, benefits the five viscera, boosts qi, and lightens the body. Grows in river marshes.
