Shanzhuyu (Cornus / Asiatic cornelian cherry). Taste sour; non-toxic. Indicated for evil qi below the heart with cold-heat; warms the middle, expels cold-damp bi, and removes the three worms. Taken long-term, lightens the body. Also called Shuzao. Grows in mountain valleys.

Wuzhuyu (evodia fruit). Taste pungent, warm. Mainly warms the middle, lowers qi, and stops pain; treats cough with rebellious qi and cold-heat, removes damp blood bi, expels wind evil, and opens the interstices (couli). The root kills the three worms. Also called Yi. Grows in river valleys.

Qinpi (ash bark). Taste sour; non-toxic. Indicated for wind-cold-damp bi and shivering chills; removes heat, and treats bluish nebula and white film in the eyes. Taken long-term, keeps the hair from turning white and lightens the body. Grows in river valleys.

Zhizi (gardenia fruit). Taste bitter, cold. Indicated for evil qi within the five viscera, heat qi in the stomach, facial redness, wine-induced pustules and rosacea, red nose, and white or red leprosy-type sores and ulcers. Also called Mudan. Grows in river valleys.

Hehuan (silk tree, Albizia). Taste sweet, neutral. Mainly calms the five viscera, harmonizes the heart and will, and makes one joyful and free of worry. Taken long-term, lightens the body, brightens the eyes, and grants one's desires. Grows in the mountain valleys of Yizhou.

Qinjiao (Sichuan pepper). Taste pungent, warm. Indicated for wind evil qi; warms the middle, removes cold bi, strengthens the teeth, lengthens the hair, and brightens the eyes. Taken long-term, lightens the body, improves the complexion, delays aging, extends the years, and clears the spirit. Grows in river valleys.

Ziwei (trumpet creeper / Campsis flower). Taste sour, slightly cold. Indicated for women's post-nursing ailments, flooding (profuse uterine bleeding), concretions and blood blockage, and cold-heat with emaciation; nourishes the fetus. Grows in river valleys.

Wuyi (elm fruit paste). Taste pungent. Indicated for evil qi within the five viscera; disperses creeping toxin moving through the skin and joints. Expels the three worms and aids digestion. Also called Wugu; an alternate name is partly illegible in the source text. Grows in river valleys.

Sanggen baipi (mulberry root bark). Taste sweet, cold. Indicated for internal injury, the five taxations and six extremities with emaciation, and flooding with pulse collapse; supplements deficiency and boosts qi. The leaf removes cold-heat and induces sweating. Mulberry ear (fungus growing on the mulberry tree), the black variety, treats women's vaginal discharge with red-and-white fluid, blood disorders, concretions and accumulations with abdominal pain, yin-yang cold-heat, and infertility. The 'five wood ears' (fungi growing on five kinds of trees), also called Ru, boost qi, stave off hunger, lighten the body, and strengthen the will.
Trees and Wood — Lower Grade

Songluo (usnea / tree-moss lichen). Taste bitter, neutral. Indicated for evil qi of anger and rage; stops deficiency sweating, treats wind in the head, and cold swelling pain in women's genitals. Also called Nüluo. Grows in mountain valleys.

Wujia (Acanthopanax). Taste pungent, warm. Indicated for hernia-qi (shan) pain in the heart and abdomen, and abdominal pain; boosts qi and treats lameness. Enables young children to stand and walk early; treats flat welling-sores and erosive sores of the genital region. Also called Chaiqi.

Zhuling (Polyporus umbellatus). Taste sweet, neutral. Indicated for malarial disorders; resolves toxin, gu-toxin and gu-infestation, and inauspicious influences; promotes urination. Taken long-term, lightens the body and delays aging. Also called Jiazhushi (lit. 'boar's dung'). Grows in mountain valleys.
Baiji (white jujube thorn). Taste pungent, cold. Indicated for heart and abdominal pain, and stops pain from welling-abscess with pus and rupture. Also called Jizhen. Grows in river valleys.

Weimao (winged spindle tree, Euonymus alatus). Taste bitter, cold; non-toxic. Indicated for women's flooding with vaginal bleeding, and abdominal fullness with sweating; dispels evil, and kills ghost-toxin and gu-infestation. Also called Guijian (ghost arrow).

Huanghuan. Taste bitter; toxic. Indicated for gu-toxin, ghost-infestation and ghost-possession, and evil qi lodged within the viscera; removes cough with rebellious qi and cold-heat. Also called Lingquan, Dajiu. Grows in mountain valleys.

Shinan (Photinia leaf) — taste pungent, neutral. Nourishes kidney qi, treats internal damage and declining yin, benefits the sinews, bones, skin and hair. The fruit kills gu-poison, breaks up masses and accumulations, and expels wind-bi. Also called Guimu ("ghost eye"). Grows in mountain valleys.

Croton seed (Ba Dou) — taste pungent, warm, toxic. Indicated for cold-damage and warm malaria with chills and fever; breaks up concretions and hardened accumulations; treats retained fluids and phlegm nodules, and distension of the abdomen with fluid swelling. Purges the five viscera and six bowels, opens obstructions, and promotes passage through the water and food pathways; removes necrotic flesh, and eliminates ghost-toxin, gu-poison and evil influences. Kills insects and fish. Also called Bajiao. Grows in river valleys.

Sichuan pepper (Shu Jiao) — taste pungent, warm. Indicated for evil qi with cough and counterflow; warms the middle; expels numbness and dead flesh of the joints and skin, and cold-damp bi pain; directs qi downward. Taken long-term, it keeps the hair from graying, lightens the body, and prolongs life. Grows in river valleys.

Mangcao — taste pungent, warm. Indicated for wind-head disorders, carbuncles and swellings, breast abscess, and hernia-like masses; dispels bound qi, scabies and itching, and deep-rooted sores. Kills insects and fish. Grows in mountain valleys.

Yu kernel (bush-cherry pit) — taste sour, neutral. Indicated for abdominal water swelling, and swelling of the face, eyes, and limbs; promotes urination. The root treats swollen gums and dental caries, and strengthens the teeth. Rhamnus (Shu Li, buckthorn) treats chills-and-fever with scrofula sores. Also called Jueli. Grows in river valleys.
Luan flower (Koelreuteria/goldenrain-tree flower) — taste bitter, cold. Indicated for eye pain with tearing and damage to the canthus; reduces eye swelling. Grows in river valleys.

Manjiao — taste bitter, warm. Indicated for wind-cold-damp bi and pain in the joints throughout the body; dispels counterflow qi in the limbs, and knee pain. Also called Shijiao ("hog pepper"). Grows in river valleys.

Leiwan (Omphalia) — taste bitter, cold. Governs killing the three worms, and expelling toxic qi and heat from the stomach; beneficial for men, not beneficial for women. Made into a rubbing ointment, it treats the hundred diseases of children. Grows in mountain valleys.
Soushu (Deutzia) — taste pungent, cold. Indicated for heat in the skin and body; dispels evil qi; stops enuresis. May be used to make a bathing decoction. Grows in river valleys.

Yaoshi root (the root of Beimu, fritillary bulb) — taste pungent, warm. Indicated for evil qi and various aching bi conditions; mends severed injuries; supplements the bone marrow. Also called Lianmu. (This is the root of Beimu.) Grows in mountain valleys.

Gleditsia pod (Zaojia, Chinese honey-locust pod) — taste pungent, salty, warm. Indicated for wind-bi and numb dead flesh, evil qi, and wind-head with tearing eyes. Opens the nine orifices; kills malevolent spirits. Grows in river valleys.

Lianshi (Melia/chinaberry fruit) — taste bitter, cold. Indicated for warm-disease and cold-damage with high fever, vexation and mania; kills the three worms; treats scabies and sores; promotes urination. (This is the fruit of the Melia tree.) Grows in mountain valleys.

Willow catkin (Liu Hua) — taste bitter, cold. Indicated for wind edema, jaundice, and facial heat with darkening. The leaves treat scabby sores in horses. The fruit treats ruptured carbuncles, expelling pus and blood. Also called Liuxu ("willow floss"). Grows in the river marshes of Langye.

Paulownia leaf (Tong Ye) — taste bitter, cold. Indicated for malignant erosive sores of the genitals. The bark treats the five kinds of hemorrhoids and kills the three worms. The flower, applied to sores on pigs and fed to them, makes them grow three times fatter. Grows in mountain valleys.

下卷.text: Insects and Animals — Superior Grade

Processed human hair (Fabi) — taste bitter, warm. Indicated for the five stranguries with impassable blockage; promotes urination; treats infantile epilepsy and adult tetany. It also has the marvelous property of transforming back into its original substance.

Dragon bone (Longgu, fossilized bone) — taste sweet, neutral. Indicated for ghost-attachment of the heart and abdomen, malevolent spirits and old demons; cough with counterflow; diarrhea with pus and blood; women's flooding and spotting; concretions and hardened masses; infantile fright-epilepsy from heat qi. The teeth treat fright-epilepsy, mania and running madness in children and adults, bound qi below the heart with inability to breathe, and various convulsions; they kill malevolent spirits. Taken long-term, it lightens the body, opens spirit-brightness, and prolongs life. Grows in mountain valleys.

Bovine bezoar (Niuhuang, ox gallstone) — taste bitter, neutral. Indicated for fright-epilepsy with chills and fever, and severe heat with mania and tetany; dispels evil and expels ghosts. The base of the ox horn treats blocked blood, static blood with pain, and women's vaginal discharge with bleeding. The marrow supplements the middle and fills the bone marrow; taken long-term, it prolongs life. The gallbladder can be made into pills. Grows in level marshes.

Musk (Shexiang) — taste pungent, warm. Governs warding off malign qi, and killing ghosts and malevolent spirits; treats warm malaria, gu-poison, epilepsy and tetany; removes the three worms. Taken long-term, it dispels evil influences and prevents nightmares and sleep paralysis. Grows in river valleys.

Bear fat (Xiong Zhi) — taste sweet, slightly cold. Indicated for wind-bi with numbness and sinew spasm; accumulations and masses in the five viscera and abdomen; chills and fever with emaciation; scalp sores and various forms of baldness; facial spots and blemishes. Taken long-term, it strengthens the will, suppresses hunger, and lightens the body. Grows in mountain valleys.

White glue (deer-antler glue, Baijiao) — taste sweet, neutral. Indicated for internal injury and exhaustion, lower-back pain with emaciation; supplements the middle and boosts qi; women's amenorrhea and infertility; stops pain and calms the fetus. Taken long-term, it lightens the body and prolongs life. Also called Deer Antler Glue (Lujiaojiao).

Donkey-hide glue (Ejiao) — taste sweet, neutral. Indicated for internal collapse of the heart and abdomen, extreme exhaustion with chills resembling malaria, lower-back and abdominal pain, aching of the four limbs, and women's vaginal bleeding; calms the fetus. Taken long-term, it lightens the body and boosts qi. Also called Chuanzhijiao.

Red rooster (Dan Xiong Ji) — taste sweet, slightly warm. Indicated for women's flooding and spotting, and red or white vaginal discharge; supplements deficiency, warms the middle, stops bleeding, opens the spirit, kills toxin, and wards off inauspicious influences. The head kills ghosts. The fat treats deafness. The intestines treat enuresis. The yellow lining of the gizzard treats diarrhea. The white droppings treat wasting-thirst and cold-damage with chills and fever. The wing feathers treat blocked menstruation. The egg removes heat and fire-sores, epilepsy and tetany, and can be made into an amber-like marvelous substance. White grubs found on chickens fatten pigs. Grows in level marshes.

Wild goose fat (Yan Fang) — taste sweet, neutral, non-toxic. Indicated for wind contracture and spasm, hemiplegia, and impeded qi flow. Taken long-term, it boosts qi, suppresses hunger, lightens the body, and resists aging. Also called Wufang. Grows in ponds and marshes.

Honey (Shimi) — taste sweet, neutral. Indicated for evil qi in the heart and abdomen, and various fright-epilepsy and tetany conditions; calms deficiencies of the five viscera; boosts qi, supplements the middle, stops pain, resolves toxin, treats the multitude of diseases, and harmonizes the hundred medicines. Taken long-term, it strengthens the will, lightens the body, suppresses hunger, and prevents aging. Grows in mountain valleys.

Beeswax (Mila) — taste sweet, slightly warm. Indicated for diarrhea with pus and blood; supplements the middle, mends severed injuries and metal wounds, boosts qi, suppresses hunger, and resists aging. Grows in mountain valleys.

Bee larvae (Fengzi) — taste sweet, neutral. Indicated for wind-head disorders; dispels gu-poison; supplements deficiency and emaciation, and internal injury. Taken long-term, it gives one a lustrous glow and good complexion, and prevents aging. The larvae of large hornets treat distension and pain of the heart and abdomen, lightening the body and boosting qi. The larvae of ground wasps treat carbuncles and swellings. Also called Feiling. Grows in mountain valleys.

Oyster shell (Muli). Taste salty, neutral. Indicated for cold-damage fever and chills, shivering in warm-type malaria, and fright and anger qi. Relieves muscle spasm and flaccidity, scrofula, and women's red and white vaginal discharge. Taken long-term, strengthens the bones and joints, wards off evil spirits, and prolongs life. Also called Ligé. Grows in ponds and marshes.

Carp gallbladder. Taste bitter, cold. Indicated for red, painful eyes from heat, and blue-blindness; brightens the eyes. Taken long-term, strengthens vigor and boosts the will and vital energy. Grows in ponds and marshes.
Snakehead fish. Taste sweet, cold. Indicated for damp bi-syndrome with facial and eye swelling; drains excess water. Also called Tong fish. Grows in ponds and marshes.
Insects and Animals — Middle Grade

Rhinoceros horn. Taste bitter, cold. Indicated for the hundred poisons and gu-toxin infestations, evil spirits and miasmic qi; neutralizes the poisons of gouwen (Gelsemium), toxic bird feathers, and snake venom. Dispels evil influences and prevents confusion and nightmares. Taken long-term, lightens the body. Grows in river valleys.

Antelope horn. Taste salty, cold. Brightens the eyes, boosts qi, and revives sexual vigor. Removes malign blood flowing downward, wards off gu-toxin, evil spirits, and inauspicious influences, calms the heart-qi, and prevents nightmares. Taken long-term, strengthens the sinews and bones and lightens the body. Grows in river valleys.

Black goat horn. Taste salty, warm. Indicated for blue-blindness; brightens the eyes, kills scabies mites, stops cold-type diarrhea, wards off evil spirits, tigers and wolves, and stops fright palpitations. Taken long-term, calms the heart, boosts qi, and lightens the body. Grows in river valleys.

White horse penis. Taste salty, neutral. Indicated for internal injury with the pulse cut off, and impotence. Strengthens the will, boosts qi, builds muscle, and promotes vigor and fertility. The eye treats fright epilepsy, abdominal fullness, and malaria. The dew-claw treats fright-evil convulsions, difficult labor, and wards off malign qi, ghosts, poison, and gu-toxin infestations. Grows in level marshes.

Dog penis. Taste salty, neutral. Indicated for internal injury and impotence; makes one strong and virile and aids conception, and removes the twelve ailments of women's vaginal discharge. The gallbladder brightens the eyes. Also called Dog Essence. Grows in level marshes.

Deer antler velvet. Taste sweet, warm. Indicated for vaginal spotting with malign blood, chills and fever, and fright epilepsy; boosts qi, strengthens the will, and promotes tooth growth and longevity. The antler (once hardened) treats malignant sores and welling-abscesses, expels evil and malign qi, and clears stagnant blood retained in the vagina.

Bat. Taste salty, neutral. Indicated for dim vision; brightens the eyes and grants clear night vision. Taken long-term, makes one joyful, charming, and carefree. Also called Bianfu. Grows in the river valleys of Mount Tai.

Hedgehog skin. Taste bitter, neutral. Indicated for the five types of hemorrhoids with genital erosion, bleeding with red and white discharge, unceasing multicolored bloody fluid, and genital swelling and pain radiating to the lower back. Kill the hedgehog by boiling it in wine before use. Grows in river valleys.

Skink. Taste salty, cold. Indicated for the five types of urinary blockage and bound evil qi; breaks up stone strangury, stops bleeding, and promotes urination. Also called Xiyi (lizard). Grows in river valleys.

Mantis egg case (Sangpiaoxiao). Taste salty, neutral. Indicated for internal injury, hernia-like masses, and impotence; boosts essence and aids conception. Treats women's amenorrhea with lower back pain, relieves the five types of painful urinary dysfunction, and promotes urination. Grows on mulberry branches; gather and steam before use. Also called Shiyou.
Cicada. Taste salty, cold. Indicated for infant fright epilepsy, night crying, epilepsy, and chills and fever. Grows on willow trees.

White stiff silkworm. Taste salty. Indicated for infant fright epilepsy and night crying; removes the three parasites, clears dark facial blemishes, improves the complexion, and treats genital sores in men. Grows in level marshes.

Wood horsefly. Taste bitter, neutral. Indicated for red, painful eyes, injured canthus with tearing, stagnant blood and amenorrhea, chills and fever with aching, and infertility. Also called Huncháng. Grows in river marshes.

Flying horsefly (Feimeng). Taste bitter, slightly cold. Expels stagnant blood, breaks down hardened blood accumulations, masses and concretions with chills and fever, and frees the blood vessels and the nine orifices. Grows in river valleys.

Feilian (a beetle-like insect). Taste salty. Indicated for blood stasis and hardened masses with chills and fever; breaks up accumulations, treats throat bi-obstruction, and internal cold with infertility. Grows in river marshes.

White grub (dung beetle larva). Taste salty, slightly warm. Indicated for malign blood and blood stasis, bi-syndrome qi; breaks up bruised blood causing hardness, fullness, and pain under the ribs, amenorrhea, and excess tissue growth in the eye with bluish nebula and white membrane. Also called Fenqi. Grows in level marshes.

Slug. Taste salty, cold. Indicated for pathogenic wind causing facial paralysis, tendon injury and rectal prolapse, and fright epilepsy with spasmodic contraction. Also called Lingli. Grows in ponds and marshes.

Sea clam. Taste bitter, neutral. Indicated for cough with rising qi, wheezing with vexation and fullness, and chest pain with chills and fever. Also called Kuige. Grows in ponds and marshes. Wenge (patterned clam) treats malignant sores and erodes the five types of hemorrhoids.

Turtle shell. Taste salty, neutral. Indicated for vaginal spotting with red and white discharge; breaks up masses and concretions, treats chronic malaria, the five types of hemorrhoids with genital erosion, damp bi-syndrome, heaviness and weakness of the limbs, and unclosed fontanelle in infants. Taken long-term, lightens the body and suppresses hunger. Also called Spirit House. Grows in ponds and marshes.

Soft-shell turtle shell. Taste salty, neutral. Indicated for masses and concretions in the chest and abdomen, hardened accumulations with chills and fever; removes epigastric fullness, polyps, genital erosion, hemorrhoids, and malignant flesh growths. Grows in ponds and marshes.

Tuo-fish scale armor (armored fish scales). Taste pungent, slightly warm. Indicated for masses and concretions in the chest and abdomen, latent hardened accumulations, chills and fever, women's flooding with multicolored uterine bleeding, pain linking the lower abdomen and genitals, scabies sores, and necrotic flesh. Grows in ponds and marshes.

Cuttlefish bone. Taste salty, slightly warm. Indicated for women's spotting with red and white menstrual discharge, amenorrhea, genital erosion with swelling and pain, chills and fever, masses and concretions, and infertility. Grows in ponds and marshes.

Crab. Taste salty, cold. Indicated for evil qi in the chest, painful heat binding, and facial paralysis with swelling. Counteracts the toxicity of lacquer. When burned, it draws out rats. Grows in ponds and marshes.

Toad. Taste pungent, cold nature. Indicated for pathogenic qi; breaks up hard masses and stagnant blood, treats abscesses, swellings, and genital sores. Taken long-term, prevents febrile disease. Grows in ponds and marshes.
Insects and Animals — Lower Grade

Hair and hoof-nails of the six domestic animals. Taste salty, neutral nature. Indicated for ghost-attachment and gu poisoning, chills and fever, fright epilepsy, and madness with convulsions and manic wandering. Camel hair is especially good.
Flying squirrel. Induces abortion and eases childbirth.

Elk fat. Taste pungent, warm nature. Indicated for abscesses, swellings, malignant sores, and necrotic flesh; cold-wind damp painful obstruction, stiffness and contracture of the four limbs, wind-swelling of the head with qi stagnation; it opens the skin's interstices. Also called gong fat. Grows in mountain valleys.

Pig testicles. Taste sweet, warm nature. Indicated for fright epilepsy and madness, ghost-attachment and gu poisoning; relieves chills and fever, running-piglet qi surges, the five stranguries, pathogenic qi, and spasmodic contraction. The dew-claw treats the five types of hemorrhoids, hidden heat lodged in the intestines, and intestinal abscess with internal erosion. Also called tún diān.

Swallow droppings. Taste pungent, neutral nature. Indicated for gu poisoning and ghost-attachment; expels inauspicious pathogenic qi, breaks the five stranguries, and disinhibits urination. Found in high valleys and level valleys.

Bat droppings. Taste pungent, cold nature. Indicated for swelling and abscess of the face and eyes, recurring skin pain, blood-qi disorders in the abdomen; breaks up accumulations from cold and heat, and relieves fright palpitations. Also called shǔgū, also called shígān (stone liver). Grows in the mountain valleys of Hepu.

Hornet's nest. Taste bitter, neutral nature. Indicated for fright epilepsy with convulsions, chills and fever from pathogenic qi, madness, ghost essence and gu poisoning, and intestinal hemorrhoids. Best prepared roasted over fire. Also called fēngchǎng. Grows in mountain valleys.

Chū jī (ailanthus lanternfly). Taste bitter, neutral nature. Indicated for pathogenic qi in the heart and abdomen, and impotence; boosts essence, strengthens willpower, aids conception, and enhances sexual vigor. Tonifies the middle and lightens the body. Grows in river valleys.

Zhè chóng (wingless ground beetle). Taste salty, cold nature. Indicated for recurring chills and fever in the heart and abdomen, blood accumulations and abdominal masses; breaks up hardness and expels blocked menstrual blood. Excellent for aiding conception. Also called dìbiē. Grows in river marshes.
Leech. Taste salty, neutral nature. Expels malign and stagnant blood and blocked menstruation; breaks up blood masses and accumulations, treats infertility, and disinhibits the waterways.

Shícán (caddisfly larva). Taste sour, non-toxic. Indicated for the five stranguries; breaks the five types of painful urinary dribbling, and induces abortion. Its flesh resolves bound qi, disinhibits the waterways, and clears heat. Also called shāshī. Grows in ponds and marshes.

Snake slough. Taste salty, neutral nature. Indicated for the 120 kinds of infantile fright epilepsy, convulsions, madness, chills and fever, intestinal hemorrhoids, worm toxicity, and snake-epilepsy. Best prepared roasted over fire. Also called lóngzǐyī, shéfú, lóngzǐdānyī, and gōngpí (bow-skin). Grows in mountain valleys.

Centipede. Taste pungent, warm nature. Indicated for ghost-attachment and gu poisoning; counteracts venom from snakes, insects, and fish, kills malevolent spirits and old specters, treats warm malaria, and expels the three worms. Grows in river valleys.

Millipede. Taste pungent, warm nature. Indicated for severe hard steaming heat in the abdomen; breaks up accumulations and masses, treats polyps, malignant sores, and scalp ringworm. Also called bǎizú (hundred-legs). Grows in river valleys.

Yìwēng (mud-dauber wasp). Taste pungent, neutral nature. Indicated for chronic deafness, cough with counterflow qi, and toxic qi; draws out embedded splinters, and induces sweating. Grows in river valleys.
Quèwèng (moth cocoon). Taste sweet, neutral nature. Indicated for infantile fright epilepsy, chills and fever with bound qi, gu poisoning, and ghost-attachment. Also called zàoshè.
Bǐzǐ. Taste sweet, warm nature. Indicated for pathogenic qi in the abdomen; expels the three worms, treats snake bites, gu poisoning, ghost-attachment, and hidden corpse (a wasting disease).

Shǔfù (pillbug/woodlouse). Taste sour, warm nature. Indicated for qi strangury with inability to urinate, blocked menstruation and blood masses in women, epilepsy and convulsions with chills and fever; disinhibits the waterways. Also called pánzhēn, also called yīxián. Grows in level valleys.

Firefly. Taste pungent, slightly warm nature. Brightens the eyes; treats fire-sore injuries in children and heat qi, gu poisoning, and ghost-attachment; opens the spirit-essence. Also called yèguāng (night light). Grows in ponds and marshes.

Silverfish. Taste salty, warm nature, non-toxic. Indicated for hernia-like abdominal masses in women, difficult urination, and infantile wind-strike with stiff neck — for all of these it should be applied as a rub. Also called báiyú (white fish). Grows in level marshes.

White-necked earthworm. Taste salty, cold nature. Indicated for snake-type abdominal masses; expels the three worms and hidden corpse, ghost-attachment and gu poisoning; kills long worms (roundworms). It can also dissolve itself into water. Grows in level ground.

Mole cricket. Taste salty, cold nature. Indicated for difficult childbirth; draws out splinters embedded in the flesh, ruptures abscesses and swellings, relieves choking obstruction in the throat, resolves toxicity, and treats malignant sores. Those caught at night are best. Also called huìgū, tiānlóu, and hú. Grows in level marshes.

Dung beetle. Taste salty, cold nature. Indicated for infantile fright epilepsy with convulsions, abdominal distension with chills and fever, and adult madness and erratic behavior. Best prepared roasted over fire. Also called jiéqiāng. Grows in ponds and marshes.

Blister beetle. Taste pungent, cold nature. Indicated for chills and fever from ghost-attachment and gu poisoning, scrofula, malignant sores, erosive ulcers, and necrotic flesh; breaks up stony strangury. Also called lóngwěi (dragon tail). Grows in river valleys.

Dìdǎn (oil beetle). Taste pungent, cold nature. Indicated for chills and fever from ghost-attachment, scrofula and malignant sores, and necrotic accumulations; breaks up abdominal masses, and induces abortion. Also called yuánqīng. Grows in river valleys.
Mǎdāo (a freshwater clam/mussel). Taste pungent, slightly cold; toxic. Indicated for metrorrhagia (abnormal uterine bleeding).
Fruits and Vegetables — Superior Grade

Grape. Taste sweet, neutral. Indicated for damp painful bi-syndrome of the sinews and bones; boosts qi, redoubles strength, strengthens the will, makes one stout and healthy, and enables one to withstand hunger, wind, and cold. Taken long-term, it lightens the body, prevents aging, and prolongs life. Can be made into wine. Grows in mountain valleys.

Péngléi (wild raspberry). Taste sour, neutral. Mainly calms the five viscera, boosts essence-qi, strengthens and firms yin, strengthens the will and redoubles strength, and aids fertility. Taken long-term, it lightens the body and prevents aging. Also called Fùpén. Grows in flat wetlands.

Chinese date (jujube). Taste sweet, neutral. Indicated for pathogenic qi of the heart and abdomen; calms the middle, nourishes the spleen, assists the twelve channels, calms stomach qi, opens the nine orifices, and supplements deficient qi and fluids, bodily insufficiency, being easily startled, and heaviness of the limbs. Harmonizes the hundred medicinals. Taken long-term, it lightens the body and lengthens life. Its leaves, combined with ephedra, can induce sweating. Grows in flat wetlands.

Lotus seed and rhizome. Taste sweet, neutral. Mainly supplements the middle, nourishes the spirit, boosts qi and strength, and eliminates the hundred diseases. Taken long-term, it lightens the body, resists aging, prevents hunger, and prolongs life. Also called Shuǐzhī ("Water Fungus"). Grows in ponds and marshes.

Qiánshí (foxnut, lit. "chicken head"). Taste sweet, neutral. Indicated for damp painful bi-syndrome, pain of the lower back, spine, and knees; supplements the middle, eliminates the hundred diseases, boosts essence-qi, strengthens the will, and sharpens hearing and vision. Taken long-term, it lightens the body, prevents hunger, and grants immortal-like longevity. Also called Yànhuì ("wild-goose beak"). Grows in ponds and marshes.
Sweet melon seed. Taste sweet, neutral. Mainly makes one glossy and radiant, gives a good complexion, and boosts qi while preventing hunger. Taken long-term, it lightens the body and resists aging.

Melon pedicle. Taste bitter, cold. Indicated for major water [edema] with swelling of the face and limbs; drains water, kills gu-poison, and treats cough with rising qi. For indigestion after eating fruit, when the ailment lies in the chest and abdomen, it induces vomiting and purging to expel it. Also called Tǔzhī. Grows in flat wetlands.

Winter mallow seed. Taste sweet, cold. Indicated for cold-heat and emaciation of the five viscera and six bowels; breaks the five types of painful urinary dysfunction and disinhibits urination. Taken long-term, it strengthens the bones, builds muscle, lightens the body, and prolongs life.

Amaranth seed. Taste sweet, cold. Indicated for blindness without visible eye damage; brightens the eyes, eliminates pathogenic influences, disinhibits urination and defecation, and clears cold-heat. Taken long-term, it boosts qi and strength, prevents hunger, and lightens the body. Also called Mǎxiàn. Grows in river wetlands.

Bitter lettuce. Taste bitter, cold. Indicated for pathogenic qi of the five viscera, and aversion to food with stomach bi-syndrome. Taken long-term, it calms the heart, boosts qi, sharpens hearing and perception, reduces the need for sleep, lightens the body, and resists aging. Also called Túcǎo, also called Xuǎn. Grows in river valleys.
Fruits and Vegetables — Middle Grade
Cherry. Taste sweet, neutral; non-toxic. Mainly regulates the middle and boosts spleen qi; gives one a good complexion and a pleasant disposition.

Mume fruit (Chinese plum). Taste sour, neutral. Mainly directs qi downward, relieves heat vexation and fullness, calms the heart, and treats pain of the limbs and body, hemiplegia with numbness, and necrotic flesh. Removes dark blemishes and malignant sores. Grows in river valleys.

Smartweed seed. Taste pungent, warm. Mainly brightens the eyes, warms the middle, and enables one to withstand wind and cold; drains water-qi, swelling of the face and eyes, and sores. Mǎliǎo (a smartweed variety) expels leeches from the intestines. Lightens the body. Grows in river wetlands.

Scallion seed. Taste pungent, warm. Mainly brightens the eyes and supplements insufficiency in the middle. Its stalk can be made into a decoction, indicated for cold-heat of cold-damage disorder, to induce sweating, and for wind-stroke with swelling of the face and eyes. Xiè (Chinese onion), taste pungent, indicated for festering wounds from metal injury. [Scallion] lightens the body, prevents hunger, and resists aging. Grows in flat wetlands.

Shuǐsū (Chinese figwort/betony). Taste pungent, slightly warm. Mainly directs qi downward and aids digestion, eliminates bad breath, removes toxicity, and dispels foul qi. Taken long-term, it opens the spirit-intelligence, lightens the body, and resists aging. Grows in ponds and marshes.

Apricot kernel. Taste sweet, warm. Indicated for cough with rising qi, rumbling [in the chest], and throat bi-syndrome; directs qi downward, aids childbirth and lactation, treats metal-wounds, cold in the heart region, and running-piglet qi. Grows in river valleys.
Fruits and Vegetables — Lower Grade

Peach kernel. Taste bitter, neutral. Indicated for stagnant blood, blood blockage, abdominal masses, and pathogenic qi; kills small parasites. Peach blossom expels possessing malevolent spirits and gives one a good complexion. Táoxiāo (a mummified peach left hanging through winter), slightly warm, kills the hundred ghosts and spirit-things. Peach fuzz treats blood masses with abnormal bleeding, cold-heat accumulations, and infertility. Peach-tree borer kills ghosts and inauspicious evil influences. Grows in mountain valleys.
Bitter gourd. Taste bitter, cold. Indicated for major water [edema] with swelling of the face and limbs; drains water. Induces vomiting. Grows in mountain wetlands.

Water celery (water dropwort). Taste sweet, neutral. Indicated for women's reddish vaginal discharge; stops bleeding, nourishes essence, protects the blood vessels, boosts qi, and makes one stout, healthy, and eager to eat. Also called Shuǐyīng. Grows in ponds and marshes.
Grains — Superior Grade

Sesame. Taste sweet, neutral; non-toxic. Indicated for injury to the middle with deficiency and emaciation; supplements the five internal organs, boosts qi and strength, builds muscle, and fills the brain marrow. Taken long-term, it lightens the body and prevents aging. Its leaf is called Qīngráng; also called Jùshèng. Grows in river valleys.

Hemp flower-fruit clusters. Taste pungent, neutral. Indicated for the seven types of injury; disinhibits the five viscera, and expels stagnant blood and cold qi. Eaten in excess, it makes one see ghosts and run about madly. Taken long-term, it opens the spirit-intelligence and lightens the body. Hemp seed, taste sweet, neutral, mainly supplements the middle and boosts qi. Taken long-term, it makes one stout and healthy and resists aging. Also called Mábó. Grows in river valleys.
Grains — Middle Grade

Fermented soybean sprout. Taste sweet, neutral; non-toxic. Indicated for damp bi-syndrome with sinew spasm and knee pain. Raw soybean is applied to sores and swelling; boiled and drunk as a decoction, it kills gu-poison and stops pain. Red adzuki bean drains water and expels pus and blood from sores and swellings. Grows in flat wetlands.

Foxtail millet. Taste bitter; non-toxic. Mainly nourishes kidney qi, removes heat from the stomach and spleen, and boosts qi. The aged grain, bitter in taste, treats stomach heat, wasting-thirst disorder, and disinhibits urination.
Glutinous millet. Taste sweet; non-toxic. Mainly boosts qi and supplements the middle; in excess it is heating and can cause vexation.
Grains — Lower Grade
Fǔbì. Taste pungent, neutral. Indicated for malarial disorders with cold-heat, pathogenic qi, and diarrhea; treats impotence and headache from wine-drinking.
